#### **Part (a) — Define an Intelligent Agent. Describe the agent-environment interaction model with a block diagram, and explain the rationality formula used for optimal action selection. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An intelligent agent is any system that perceives its environment through sensors and acts upon it through actuators, with the objective of maximizing a defined performance measure.

**Agent-Environment Interaction (Block Diagram):**

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
        │          AGENT                   │
        │                                  │
   [Sensors] ──→ [Percept] ──→ [Internal   │      ┌────────────────┐
                    │            Function] │ ──→  │   Action       │
                    │                       │      └───────┬────────┘
                    └──────── [State] ──────┘              │
                                                          ▼
                                                  [Actuators]
                                                          │
                                                          ▼
                                                  [ENVIRONMENT]
                                                          │
   [Sensors] ←──────────────────────────────────────  [State Output]
        └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Rationality Formula:**

For a percept sequence $\sigma$ and available actions $\mathcal{A}$, the rational action is:

$$
a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; \mathbb{E}[V \vert \sigma, a] = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in S} P(s' \vert \sigma, a) \cdot V(s')
$$

This formula incorporates:
- **$P(s' \vert \sigma, a)$** — the agent's probabilistic model of the world's response to its action
- **$V(s')$** — the agent's preference over states (the performance measure)
- **$\arg\max$** — the action selection rule

A rational agent picks the action that maximizes the **expected** future performance — not the **actual** one (since the actual outcome is uncertain in stochastic environments).

---

#### **Part (b) — Specify the PEAS description for: (i) Automated Teller Machine (ATM) Agent, (ii) Chess-Playing AI. [7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) ATM Agent — PEAS Description:**

| **PEAS Element**         | **Specification**                                                                          |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Performance Measure**  | Successful transactions, customer satisfaction, security, accuracy, low error rate         |
| **Environment**          | Customers, bank server, cash dispenser, card reader, network connection, security cameras  |
| **Actuators**            | Display screen, cash dispenser, receipt printer, speaker, card ejector, key dispenser     |
| **Sensors**              | Card reader, keypad/PIN pad, touchscreen, camera, network interface, cash sensor          |

**Environment Properties for ATM:** Fully observable (all relevant info is on screen), Deterministic (actions have predictable effects), Episodic (each transaction is independent), Static (does not change during deliberation), Discrete, Single-agent, Known.

**(ii) Chess-Playing AI — PEAS Description:**

| **PEAS Element**         | **Specification**                                                                          |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Performance Measure**  | Win rate, ELO rating, time per move, points captured, minimizing opponent's options       |
| **Environment**          | Chessboard (8x8 grid), opponent (human or another AI), clock, tournament rules            |
| **Actuators**            | Move generator (piece movement on board, or digital output to GUI)                        |
| **Sensors**              | Board state (piece positions), opponent's move input, clock reading, opening book access  |

**Environment Properties for Chess:** Fully observable (both players see the entire board), Deterministic (each move has a definite next state), Sequential (each move affects the position), Static (board does not change while the agent thinks), Discrete, Multi-agent (two players competing), Known (rules are fully specified).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Both the ATM and chess AI are *fully observable, deterministic, and known* — but they differ in whether they are *episodic* (ATM) versus *sequential* (chess). This is why the ATM can be implemented as a simple reflex agent, while a chess AI must be goal-based or learning-based.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (READ CAREFULLY):**
> 1. **Conflating rationality with omniscience** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you claim a rational agent must "know everything." Rationality = expected maximum, not actual maximum.
> 2. **Confusing the agent function with the agent program** — The function is an *abstract mapping* $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \to \mathcal{A}$; the program is the *concrete implementation* running on hardware.
> 3. **Skipping the world model in a Model-Based Reflex Agent** — Many students draw a diagram but omit the "State Update" or "World Model" block, which is the *defining feature* of this agent type. Always include it.
> 4. **PEAS incomplete** — Listing only 2 or 3 of the 4 PEAS elements. Examiners expect all four explicitly named.
> 5. **Mixing up episodic with static** — These are independent properties. An episodic environment is *not necessarily* static (e.g., a radar system is episodic but dynamic).
> 6. **Writing the rationality formula without defining variables** — Always define $V$, $P$, $\sigma$, and $a$ before using them in the equation.
> 7. **Forgetting diagrams in Part B** — A 14-mark question on "agent architectures" with no diagram will lose 2–3 marks automatically. Always include at least one labelled block diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1:**

### **Core Definitions (MUST memorize)**
- ✅ **Agent** = sensors + actuators + agent function
- ✅ **Rational Agent** = selects action maximizing *expected* performance measure
- ✅ **PEAS** = Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors
- ✅ **Agent Function** $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \rightarrow \mathcal{A}$
- ✅ **Rationality Formula** $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert \sigma, a) \cdot V(s')$

### **Seven Environment Properties (must know all)**
- ✅ Observability — Fully / Partially
- ✅ Determinism — Deterministic / Stochastic
- ✅ Episodicity — Episodic / Sequential
- ✅ Dynamism — Static / Dynamic
- ✅ Continuity — Discrete / Continuous
- ✅ Agency — Single-agent / Multi-agent
- ✅ Knowledge — Known / Unknown

### **Five Agent Architectures (with one-line summaries)**
- ✅ **Simple Reflex** = *condition-action rules only*
- ✅ **Model-Based Reflex** = *internal state + world model + rules*
- ✅ **Goal-Based** = *world model + goals + planning*
- ✅ **Utility-Based** = *world model + utility function + decision*
- ✅ **Learning Agent** = *performance element + critic + learning element + problem generator*

### **Canonical Examples (high-yield)**
- ✅ Vacuum Cleaner World — Simple Reflex / Model-Based Reflex
- ✅ Automated Taxi Driver — Utility-Based / Learning Agent
- ✅ Medical Diagnosis System — Goal-Based / Utility-Based
- ✅ Chess AI — Goal-Based with adversarial search
- ✅ ATM — Simple Reflex with episodic transactions
- ✅ Refinery Controller — Utility-Based with real-time decisions

### **Equations You Must Be Able to Write on the Board**
- ✅ $f : \mathcal{P}^{*} \to \mathcal{A}$
- ✅ $a^{*} = \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \mathbb{E}[V \vert \sigma, a]$
- ✅ $\mathbb{E}[V] = \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert \sigma, a) \cdot V(s')$
- ✅ $V_{\text{disc}} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{t} \cdot r_{t}$

### **Conceptual Distinctions (favourite exam questions)**
- ✅ Rational ≠ Omniscient
- ✅ Agent Function ≠ Agent Program
- ✅ Episodic ≠ Static
- ✅ Fully Observable ≠ Deterministic
- ✅ Percept ≠ State
- ✅ Performance Measure ≠ Utility Function
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Problem Solving by Searching: Problem Solving Agents and Examples, Searching for Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Problem Solving by Searching: Problem Solving Agents and Examples, Searching for Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Problem-Solving Agent?

A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a special class of **rational, goal-based agent** in Artificial Intelligence that, when faced with a complex goal it cannot achieve through reflex or pre-defined rules, employs **atomic representations** of its environment and systematically explores a sequence of possible actions to find an optimal or satisfactory path from the current state to a desired goal state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A problem-solving agent is a goal-based agent that uses atomic representation and decides what to do by finding sequences of actions that lead to desirable states. It uses search algorithms as a systematic problem-solving methodology."

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is foundational because it introduces the **search formulation** paradigm — the bridge between symbolic AI and classical AI. The agent does not know the entire solution upfront; it must **formulate**, **search**, and **execute**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The GPS Navigation System

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi** to **Thiruvananthapuram** in Kerala. You do not have a pre-programmed reflex telling you exactly which U-turn to take at every junction. Instead:

1. You **formulate a goal**: "Reach Thiruvananthapuram."
2. You **examine the current state**: "I am currently in Kochi with a full tank."
3. You **consider possible actions**: "Take NH-66, take MC Road, take local roads."
4. You **search for a sequence of actions** (the route) using a map.
5. You **execute the actions** while monitoring the environment.

This is exactly how a problem-solving agent works in AI — the **map of Kerala is the state space**, **cities are states**, **roads are actions**, and the **shortest route is the solution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A problem-solving agent does not "know" the answer. It **constructs** the answer through systematic exploration. The intelligence lies in the *search strategy*, not in memorized solutions.

---

## 1.3 Why is This Topic a "High-Yield" Module for KTU?

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme PCAIT302 syllabus, this is **Module 2** and forms the basis for:

- **Uninformed Search** (BFS, DFS, UCS, DLS, IDS)
- **Informed Search** (Greedy Best-First, A*, AO*)
- **Adversarial Search** (Minimax, Alpha-Beta)
- **Constraint Satisfaction Problems**

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently frame questions like *"Define a problem in terms of what an agent can do and how the world works"* (3 marks) or *"Formulate the 8-puzzle problem"* (7 marks). Mastering the **5 components of problem formulation** is non-negotiable.

---

## 1.4 The Four Phases of a Problem-Solving Agent

The classical AI agent loop for problem solving consists of four interleaved phases:

| Phase | Description | Real-World Analogy |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Goal Formulation** | Define the target state(s) | "I want to reach Trivandrum" |
| **Problem Formulation** | Define the abstract world model | "Map of Kerala with cities and roads" |
| **Search** | Explore possible action sequences | "Calculate possible routes on GPS" |
| **Execute** | Perform the chosen actions in the real world | "Drive the chosen route" |

The formal transition between these phases defines the agent's intelligence.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Components of a Well-Defined Problem

A problem in AI is defined formally as a **5-tuple** $(S, A, s_0, G, c)$ where:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST MEMORIZE — Standard KTU Board Question!**
> Every problem must have these **5 components**:

1. **Initial State** ($s_0$): The starting point from which the agent begins. Example: "Agent is in Arad (Romania problem)."

2. **Actions / Successor Function** ($A(s)$): A description of the possible actions available to the agent in a given state $s$. Formally: $A : S \to 2^A$ maps each state to a set of actions.

3. **Transition Model** (RESULT$(s, a) \to s'$): A function that describes what each action does. Given a state $s$ and an action $a$, it returns the resulting state $s'$.

4. **Goal Test** ($G$): A boolean function that checks whether a given state satisfies the goal condition. May be implicit (a set of goal states) or explicit (a property).

5. **Path Cost Function** ($c(s, a, s')$): A function that assigns a numeric cost to each step. The cost of a path is the sum of step costs:
$$\text{cost}(\text{path}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)$$

---

## 2.2 The Search Process — Formal Definition

A **search algorithm** takes a problem as input and returns a **solution** — a sequence of actions leading from the initial state to a goal state. The output is:

$$\text{Solution} = \langle a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots, a_n \rangle$$

such that:

$$\text{RESULT}(\text{RESULT}(s_0, a_1), a_2) = s_n \in G$$

**Optimal Solution**: A solution with the **minimum path cost** among all possible solutions.

---

## 2.3 The Node Data Structure (KTU High-Yield)

To keep track of the search, the algorithm creates **search nodes** (not states — this is a critical distinction examiners test).

A **Node** contains the following five fields:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `STATE` | State | The state the node corresponds to |
| `PARENT` | Node | The node in the search tree that generated this node |
| `ACTION` | Action | The action that was applied to the parent to reach this node |
| `PATH-COST` | Number | The cost of the path from initial state to this node, denoted $g(n)$ |
| `DEPTH` | Integer | The number of steps from initial state to this node |

### Key Formulas

The **path cost** of a node $n$ generated by applying action $a$ from parent node $p$ is:

$$g(n) = g(p) + c(s_p, a, s_n)$$

where:
- $g(n)$ is the path cost of node $n$
- $g(p)$ is the path cost of the parent node $p$
- $c(s_p, a, s_n)$ is the step cost from parent state via action $a$ to the new state

The **path reconstruction** (extracting the solution) is:

$$\text{Solution}(n) = \text{Solution}(\text{PARENT}(n)) \cdot \langle \text{ACTION}(n) \rangle$$

> [!NOTE]
> This recursion terminates at the root node where PARENT = **none** and PATH-COST = **0**.

---

## 2.4 The Frontier (Open List)

A **frontier** (also called the **open list** or **agenda**) is the collection of nodes that have been generated but not yet expanded.

- The **choice of data structure** for the frontier determines the search strategy.
- A **FIFO queue** → Breadth-First Search
- A **LIFO stack** → Depth-First Search
- A **priority queue ordered by path cost** → Uniform-Cost Search
- A **priority queue ordered by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$** → A* Search

---

## 2.5 Tree Search vs. Graph Search

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common KTU Mistake:** Confusing Tree Search with Graph Search. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark question!

| Feature | Tree Search | Graph Search |
|---------|-------------|--------------|
| **Repeated states** | Allowed (can loop) | Avoided (uses explored set) |
| **Data structure** | Frontier only | Frontier + **Explored set (Closed list)** |
| **Completeness** | May be incomplete due to loops | Complete for finite graphs |
| **Optimality** | Depends on algorithm | Depends on algorithm |
| **Memory** | Less | More |

A **state** can appear in the search tree as **multiple paths** (different nodes with the same state). In a graph, each state appears **only once**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 Scheme**

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol |
|---------|----------------------|--------|
| Problem 5-tuple | $(S, A, s_0, G, c)$ | $S$ = state space, $A$ = actions |
| Transition Model | $\text{RESULT}(s, a) = s'$ | Maps state + action → new state |
| Path Cost | $g(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)$ | $g(n)$ = cost from root to $n$ |
| Step Cost | $c(s, a, s')$ | Numeric cost of one transition |
| BFS Branching Factor | $b$ | Max successors per node |
| BFS Nodes at depth $d$ | $b^d$ | Exponential growth |
| Effective Branching Factor | $b^*$ | Branching factor for an N-node tree: $N = 1 + b^* + (b^*)^2 + \cdots + (b^*)^d$ |
| Solution Cost (optimal) | $C^*$ | Minimum path cost to goal |
| Solution Depth | $d$ | Number of steps in solution |
| Max Depth | $m$ | Maximum depth of state space |
| Goal Test Complexity | $O(1)$ | Constant time check |
| Successor Generation | $O(b)$ | Per node |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

Problem-solving search is used in:

1. **Route Planning (Google Maps, Ola, Uber)** — Romania problem variant with weighted roads.
2. **Robotic Path Planning (Boston Dynamics, NASA Mars Rovers)** — 8-puzzle variant in 2D/3D grid.
3. **VLSI Chip Design** — Routing wires on a chip to minimize area.
4. **Puzzle Solvers (Rubik's Cube)** — State space of $4.3 \times 10^{19}$ states.
5. **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — Logistics, supply chain optimization.
6. **Game AI (Chess, Go)** — Search through game trees.
7. **Automatic Theorem Proving** — Search through proof space.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Romania Vacation Problem

This is the **canonical example** from Russell & Norvig and appears in nearly every KTU exam. Let us derive its complete formulation.

**Problem Statement:** *A tourist is in Arad, Romania. He wants to reach Bucharest. He can drive between cities. Find the shortest route.*

### Step 1: Goal Formulation

$$\text{Goal} = \{\text{Be in Bucharest}\}$$

This is a **singleton goal set** because the goal is to be in exactly one state.

### Step 2: Problem Formulation — Initial State

$$s_0 = \text{In}(\text{Arad})$$

The agent starts in Arad. This is the only state the agent knows it is in.

### Step 3: Actions / Successor Function

$$A(\text{In}(\text{Arad})) = \{\text{Go}(\text{Sibiu}), \text{Go}(\text{Timisoara}), \text{Go}(\text{Zerind})\}$$

In general, the action set is defined by road connections in the Romanian map.

### Step 4: Transition Model (RESULT)

For example:
$$\text{RESULT}(\text{In}(\text{Arad}), \text{Go}(\text{Sibiu})) = \text{In}(\text{Sibiu})$$

### Step 5: Goal Test

$$\text{GOAL-TEST}(s) = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } s = \text{In}(\text{Bucharest}) \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 6: Path Cost Function

The cost is **distance in kilometers** (non-negative, sum of step costs):

$$c(s, a, s') = \text{RoadDist}(s, s') \geq 0$$

The total path cost is:

$$\text{cost}(\langle s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_n \rangle) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{RoadDist}(s_{i-1}, s_i)$$

### Step 7: Solution Identification

A **solution** is a sequence of cities (states) such that:
- The first city is Arad
- The last city is Bucharest
- Each consecutive pair is connected by a road

The **optimal solution** minimizes total road distance. The standard textbook answer is:

$$\text{Arad} \to \text{Sibiu} \to \text{Rimnicu Vilcea} \to \text{Pitesti} \to \text{Bucharest}$$

with total cost:

$$c^* = 140 + 80 + 97 + 101 = 418 \text{ km}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The 8-Puzzle Problem

The 8-puzzle is a **sliding tile puzzle** on a 3×3 grid with 8 numbered tiles and 1 blank space.

### Step 1: States

A state describes the location of each of the 8 tiles and the blank. The number of reachable states is:

$$N = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440$$

(The division by 2 is because half the permutations are unreachable from any given start state due to parity.)

### Step 2: Initial State

Any one of the 181,440 states can be the initial state. For example:

$$s_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & \_ & 6 \\ 8 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 3: Actions

The actions are movements of the blank space: $\{Up, Down, Left, Right\}$. The number of available actions depends on the blank's position:
- **Corner**: 2 actions
- **Edge**: 3 actions
- **Center**: 4 actions

### Step 4: Transition Model

$$\text{RESULT}(s, a) = s' \text{ where blank moves in direction } a$$

### Step 5: Goal Test

$$\text{GOAL-TEST}(s) = (s = s_{\text{goal}})$$

where $s_{\text{goal}}$ is the ordered configuration:

$$s_{\text{goal}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & \_ \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 6: Path Cost

$$c(s, a, s') = 1 \text{ for every step}$$

Each move costs 1. The path cost equals the number of moves (solution depth $d$).

### Step 7: Optimal Solution Length

The average optimal solution length is approximately:

$$\bar{d} \approx 22 \text{ moves}$$

with worst-case:

$$d_{\max} = 31 \text{ moves}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: The 8-Queens Problem

The problem: Place 8 queens on a chessboard such that no two queens attack each other.

### Step 1: Incremental vs. Complete-State Formulation

| Formulation | Initial State | Actions | Goal Test |
|-------------|---------------|---------|-----------|
| **Incremental** | Empty board | Place 1 queen at a time | 8 queens on board, none attacking |
| **Complete-State** | 8 random queens | Move any queen to another square | No two queens attacking |

### Step 2: States

- Incremental: All arrangements of 0 to 8 queens on the board.
- Complete-State: All arrangements of 8 queens on the board.

### Step 3: Actions

- Incremental: Add a queen to any empty square.
- Complete-State: Move any queen to any other square.

### Step 4: Goal Test

For incremental: exactly 8 queens on the board, no two attacking.
For complete-state: no two queens attacking.

### Step 5: Path Cost

Path cost is **irrelevant** — only the goal state matters. This is a **constraint satisfaction problem** variant.

The number of possible states in complete-state formulation:

$$N = \binom{64}{8} = 4{,}426{,}165{,}368$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Branch pruning**: If we use the "no two queens in same column" heuristic, the effective branching factor reduces to **8! = 40,320** states.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Toy Problems

| Problem | States | Goal Test | Path Cost | Notable Property |
|---------|--------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Romania | Multi-state | Singleton | Road distance (km) | Real-world weighted graph |
| 8-Puzzle | 181,440 | Singleton | 1 per move | Undirected, parity-constrained |
| 8-Queens | $\binom{64}{8} \approx 4.4 \times 10^9$ | Boolean (no attacks) | Irrelevant | Constraint satisfaction |
| Vacuum World | 8 | Singleton | 1 per action | Smallest non-trivial problem |
| Rubik's Cube | $4.3 \times 10^{19}$ | Singleton | 1 per move | God's number = 20 |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: The Tree-Search Algorithm

The general **TREE-SEARCH** algorithm is the foundation. Let us derive it formally.

**Input:** A problem $(\text{InitialState}, \text{Actions}, \text{Result}, \text{GoalTest}, \text{PathCost})$

**Output:** A solution node or failure

### Derivation Steps:

1. **Initialize** the frontier using the initial node $n_0$:
   - $\text{STATE}(n_0) = s_0$
   - $\text{PARENT}(n_0) = \text{none}$
   - $\text{PATH-COST}(n_0) = 0$
   - $\text{DEPTH}(n_0) = 0$

2. **Loop** until solution found or frontier empty:
   - If frontier is empty → return **failure** (no solution exists)
   - Choose a leaf node $n$ for expansion (strategy-specific)
   - If $n$ satisfies GOAL-TEST → return $n$ (solution)
   - **Expand** node $n$: generate successors using:
   
$$A(n) = \{a : a \in \text{Actions}(\text{STATE}(n))\}$$

   - For each action $a$, create child node $n'$:
     - $\text{STATE}(n') = \text{RESULT}(\text{STATE}(n), a)$
     - $\text{PARENT}(n') = n$
     - $\text{ACTION}(n') = a$
     - $\text{PATH-COST}(n') = g(n) + c(s_n, a, s_{n'})$
     - $\text{DEPTH}(n') = \text{DEPTH}(n) + 1$

3. **Add** all child nodes $n'$ to the frontier.

### Why a Loop?

The loop invariant is: *the frontier contains nodes whose paths have not yet been explored*. Each iteration either returns a goal (success) or expands knowledge by one level. The algorithm terminates when either condition is met.

---

## 3.6 The GRAPH-SEARCH Variant — Derivation

**Modification:** Maintain an **explored set** (closed list) $E$.

1. Initialize frontier with $n_0$, explored set $\emptyset$.
2. If frontier empty → return failure.
3. Choose leaf node $n$ from frontier.
4. If $n$ is a goal → return $n$.
5. Add $\text{STATE}(n)$ to explored set.
6. Expand $n$, but for each child $n'$:
   - If $\text{STATE}(n')$ is not in frontier and not in explored set → add to frontier.
   - Else, check if a better path exists via $n$ and update if so.

**Mathematical Justification:** If $\text{STATE}(n')$ is already in the frontier with a higher $g$-cost, replace it with the new cheaper path. This guarantees **optimality** for uniform-cost search.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Problem-Solving Agent. Mention its four phases.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | CO2, Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Problem-Solving Agent** is a goal-based agent that uses **atomic representation** of the world and decides what to do by **finding sequences of actions** that lead from the current state to a goal state.

The four phases are:

1. **Goal Formulation** — Define what the agent wants to achieve.
2. **Problem Formulation** — Define the abstract model: initial state, actions, transition model, goal test, path cost.
3. **Search** — Find a sequence of actions (a solution) by exploring the state space.
4. **Execution** — Perform the actions in the real world and monitor the environment.

**Key Formula:**
$$\text{Agent} = \text{Goal} + \text{Problem} + \text{Search} + \text{Execute}$$

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between Tree Search and Graph Search?
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | 3 Marks | CO2, Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tree Search | Graph Search |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Repeated states** | Allowed — may revisit states | Avoided using an **explored set** |
| **Data structures** | Only a **frontier** (open list) | Frontier **+ Explored set** (closed list) |
| **Completeness** | May fail in finite graphs due to loops | Complete for finite graphs |
| **Memory** | Lower | Higher |
| **Best used when** | State space is a tree or loops are rare | State space has cycles |

> A **graph search** remembers all previously visited states and prevents redundant work, while a **tree search** may generate the same state multiple times.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Explain the components of a well-defined problem. Formulate the 8-puzzle problem with all five components.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks (a) 7 + (b) 7 | CO2, Bloom Level: Apply]**

#### Part (a) — Components of a Well-Defined Problem (7 Marks)

A problem in AI is formally defined as a 5-tuple:

$$\text{Problem} = (S, A, s_0, G, c)$$

**The Five Components:**

1. **Initial State ($s_0$):** The state in which the agent starts. Example: "Agent is in Arad."

2. **Actions / Successor Function $A(s)$:** For each state $s$, a set of actions available to the agent. Formally, $A : S \to 2^A$.

3. **Transition Model $\text{RESULT}(s, a)$:** A function describing the result of applying action $a$ in state $s$, returning a new state $s'$.

4. **Goal Test $G(s)$:** A boolean function that returns True if the state $s$ satisfies the goal condition.

5. **Path Cost Function $c(s, a, s')$:** A non-negative numeric cost of taking action $a$ in state $s$ to reach $s'$. The total path cost is:
   
$$g(\text{path}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c(s_{i-1}, a_i, s_i)$$

**Worked Example — Vacuum World:**

- $S$: $\{ \text{CleanLeft, CleanRight, DirtyBoth} \}$
- $s_0$: Arbitrary starting configuration
- $A(\text{DirtyLeft})$: $\{ \text{Suck, Right, Left} \}$
- $\text{RESULT}$: Standard world rules
- $G(s)$: $s$ has both rooms clean
- $c$: 1 per action

#### Part (b) — Formulation of the 8-Puzzle Problem (7 Marks)

The 8-puzzle is a 3×3 grid with 8 numbered tiles and one blank space.

| Component | Formulation |
|-----------|-------------|
| **Initial State ($s_0$)** | Any permutation of 8 tiles + blank, e.g., $s_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 7 & 2 & 4 \\ 5 & \text{\_} & 6 \\ 8 & 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Actions $A(s)$** | $\{Up, Down, Left, Right\}$ — moves of the blank into adjacent cell |
| **Transition Model** | $\text{RESULT}(s, a) = s'$ where blank has moved in direction $a$ |
| **Goal Test $G(s)$** | $s = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & \text{\_} \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Path Cost** | $c(s, a, s') = 1$ for every move (each step costs 1) |

**State Space Size:**

$$|S| = \frac{9!}{2} = 181{,}440 \text{ reachable states}$$

**Average Solution Depth:**

$$\bar{d} \approx 22 \text{ moves}$$

---

### Question B: With a neat diagram, explain the general Tree-Search algorithm. Discuss how the choice of frontier data structure leads to different search strategies.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | 14 Marks (a) 7 + (b) 7 | CO2, Bloom Level: Analyze]**

#### Part (a) — Tree-Search Algorithm (7 Marks)

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
function TREE-SEARCH(problem) returns a solution node or failure
    initialize the frontier using the initial node of problem
    loop do
        if EMPTY(frontier) then return failure
        choose a leaf node n for expansion using QUEUEING-FN
        if GOAL-TEST(problem, STATE(n)) then return n
        expand node n: generate successors
            add successor nodes to frontier
            using QUEUEING-FN
    end
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

1. **Initialize** the frontier with the root node containing the initial state.
2. **Check** if frontier is empty. If yes, return **failure** (no solution).
3. **Select** a leaf node $n$ from the frontier (using queueing function).
4. **Test** if $n$ is a goal state. If yes, return $n$ as the solution.
5. **Expand** $n$ by applying all possible actions to generate successors.
6. **Add** the successors to the frontier using the queueing function.
7. **Repeat** from step 2.

**Node Data Structure:**

| Field | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| `STATE` | State in the state space |
| `PARENT` | Node that generated this node |
| `ACTION` | Action taken from parent |
| `PATH-COST` | $g(n) = $ total cost from root to $n$ |
| `DEPTH` | Number of steps from root to $n$ |

#### Part (b) — Frontier Data Structure and Search Strategies (7 Marks)

| Data Structure | Extraction Order | Resulting Search | Time | Space | Optimal? |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|------|-------|----------|
| **FIFO Queue** | First-In-First-Out | **BFS** (Breadth-First) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ | Yes (unit cost) |
| **LIFO Stack** | Last-In-First-Out | **DFS** (Depth-First) | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ | No |
| **Priority Queue by $g(n)$** | Lowest cost first | **UCS** (Uniform-Cost) | $O(b^{C^*/\epsilon})$ | $O(b^{C^*/\epsilon})$ | Yes |
| **Priority Queue by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$** | Lowest $f$-score | **A* Search** | Exponential | Exponential | Yes (with admissible $h$) |

where:
- $b$ = branching factor
- $d$ = depth of shallowest goal
- $m$ = maximum depth of search space
- $C^*$ = cost of optimal solution
- $\epsilon$ = minimum step cost
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of cost from $n$ to goal

**Critical Distinction:**

- The **algorithm logic is identical** — only the **QUEUEING-FN** changes.
- BFS uses FIFO; DFS uses LIFO; UCS uses min-$g$; Greedy uses min-$h$; A* uses min-$f$.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting one of the 5 components** of problem formulation (especially **path cost**). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for each missing component.
>
> 2. **Confusing State and Node** — A **state** is a configuration of the world; a **Node** is a data structure in the search tree containing a state plus path information. This distinction is worth **2 marks** in long answers.
>
> 3. **Writing "search finds a solution"** without mentioning **path cost** or **optimality** — the search may return a non-optimal solution unless the strategy guarantees optimality.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the difference between "search" and "execution"** — Search is offline (thinking); Execution is online (acting). Examiners often ask this as a 2-mark sub-question.
>
> 5. **Using $s_0$ instead of "Initial State"** in formal definitions — the KTU valuation key requires the **full terminology**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the path cost formula** $g(n) = g(p) + c(s, a, s')$ — a frequent 3-mark question.
>
> 7. **In Roman numerals (8-puzzle), not mentioning parity** — only half the permutations are reachable, hence $9!/2$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ A **problem-solving agent** is a goal-based agent using atomic representation.
- ☐ The **four phases**: Goal Formulation → Problem Formulation → Search → Execution.
- ☐ A **problem** is a 5-tuple: $(S, A, s_0, G, c)$.

### The Five Components
- ☐ **Initial State** $s_0$ — Where the agent starts.
- ☐ **Actions** $A(s)$ — What the agent can do in state $s$.
- ☐ **Transition Model** RESULT$(s, a)$ — What each action does.
- ☐ **Goal Test** $G(s)$ — Boolean: is the goal achieved?
- ☐ **Path Cost** $c(s, a, s')$ — Numeric cost per step.

### Key Data Structures
- ☐ **Node** = $\{$ STATE, PARENT, ACTION, PATH-COST, DEPTH $\}$.
- ☐ **Frontier** (open list) = nodes generated but not expanded.
- ☐ **Explored set** (closed list) = states already visited (graph search only).

### Algorithms
- ☐ **Tree Search** allows repeated states; **Graph Search** does not.
- ☐ **FIFO Queue** → BFS; **LIFO Stack** → DFS; **Priority Queue** → UCS / A*.

### Toy Problems to Remember
- ☐ **Romania** — weighted graph, real road distances.
- ☐ **8-Puzzle** — 181,440 states, unit cost.
- ☐ **8-Queens** — constraint satisfaction, 4.4 × 10⁹ states.
- ☐ **Vacuum World** — smallest non-trivial problem (8 states).

### Important Formulas
- ☐ Path cost: $g(n) = g(p) + c(s_p, a, s_n)$
- ☐ Solution cost (optimal): $C^*$
- ☐ BFS branching: $b^d$ nodes at depth $d$
- ☐ 8-puzzle state space: $9!/2 = 181{,}440$

### High-Frequency KTU Questions
- ☐ "Define a problem in terms of the 5 components" (3–7 marks).
- ☐ "Formulate the 8-puzzle / 8-queens / Romania problem" (7 marks).
- ☐ "Compare Tree Search vs Graph Search" (3–7 marks).
- ☐ "Explain the role of the frontier in search" (3 marks).
- ☐ "What is the difference between state and node?" (2–3 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uninformed Search Strategies - Breadth First Search, Uniform Cost Search, Depth First Search, Depth Limited Search, Iterative Deepening DFS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uninformed Search Strategies - Breadth First Search, Uniform Cost Search, Depth First Search, Depth Limited Search, Iterative Deepening DFS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Uninformed (Blind) Search — The Foundational Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** *Uninformed Search* (also called *Blind Search* or *Brute-Force Search*) is a class of generic state-space search algorithms that operate **without any domain-specific knowledge** beyond the problem definition. They generate successors and distinguish a goal state from a non-goal state using **only the structure of the problem graph** — no heuristic estimate $h(n)$ is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (DO NOT confuse in exams):**
> - **Uninformed Search** → No $h(n)$ used. Only $g(n)$ (path cost so far) and structure.
> - **Informed Search** (Module 3) → Uses $h(n)$ heuristic (A*, Greedy Best-First, etc.).

The five algorithms covered in KTU Module 2 are:

| # | Algorithm | Abbreviation | Frontier Data Structure |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Breadth-First Search | BFS | FIFO Queue |
| 2 | Uniform-Cost Search | UCS | Priority Queue (sorted by $g(n)$) |
| 3 | Depth-First Search | DFS | LIFO Stack |
| 4 | Depth-Limited Search | DLS | LIFO Stack (with depth cap $\ell$) |
| 5 | Iterative Deepening DFS | IDDFS | Repeated DLS with growing $\ell$ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Five Detectives"

Imagine five detectives searching a **mansion with infinite rooms** to find a hidden diamond:

- **BFS (The Sociable Detective):** Walks the mansion **floor by floor**, interviewing every room on Floor 1 before stepping onto Floor 2. *Never gets lost, but explores EVERYTHING nearby first.*
- **UCS (The Economist Detective):** Same as BFS, but **always takes the cheapest path so far** to reach the next room. If a "taxi" costs more than walking, walking is preferred.
- **DFS (The Tunnel Rat):** Picks a corridor and **dives deep** along it until hitting a dead-end, then backtracks. *Fast on lucky branches, terrible on unlucky ones — can wander forever.*
- **DLS (The Tunnel Rat with a Rope):** Same as DFS, but the detective's rope is **only $\ell$ meters long**. Beyond that, the tunnel is declared "too deep."
- **IDDFS (The Cautious Tunnel Rat):** Tries DFS with a 1-meter rope, then a 2-meter rope, then 3-meter… until diamond is found. *Combines the best of BFS and DFS.*

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (used throughout the module)

- **Branching factor ($b$):** Maximum number of successors of any node.
- **Depth of shallowest goal ($d$):** Number of edges from root to nearest goal.
- **Maximum depth of state space ($m$):** Maximum path length in the tree.
- **Depth limit ($\ell$):** The cap imposed on DLS / IDDFS.
- **Optimal path cost ($C^*$):** Cost of the cheapest goal path.
- **Minimum step cost ($\varepsilon$):** Smallest edge cost in the graph.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this:** All complexity formulas in Section 2 will use exactly these symbols. In KTU answer sheets, **always define $b$, $d$, $m$, $\ell$, $C^*$, $\varepsilon$ before substituting** — examiners award 1 mark for proper variable definition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS)

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**
1. Initialize frontier as a FIFO queue containing only the start node $S$.
2. If queue is empty → return **failure**.
3. Pop the **shallowest** (oldest) node $n$ from the queue.
4. If $n$ is goal → return **success** (path from $S$ to $n$).
5. Otherwise, **expand** $n$: generate all successors and append them to the right of the queue.
6. **Mark visited nodes** to avoid loops (use a closed/explored set).
7. Goto step 2.

**The "Why":** Because the queue is FIFO, every node at depth $d$ is expanded **before** any node at depth $d+1$. This guarantees BFS finds the shallowest goal first.

**Real-world engineering utility:** BFS underpins **shortest-path routing in unweighted networks**, **garbage collection in compilers (mark-sweep)**, **peer-to-peer file discovery (BitTorrent)**, and **social-network friend-of-friend lookups (LinkedIn degree-2 connections)**.

---

## 2.2 Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)

**Operational Logic (Dijkstra's algorithm on unweighted state space):**
1. Initialize frontier as a **priority queue** keyed by path cost $g(n)$, containing only $S$ with $g(S)=0$.
2. If priority queue is empty → return **failure**.
3. Pop node $n$ with the **lowest $g(n)$**.
4. If $n$ is goal → return **success**.
5. Otherwise, expand $n$ and insert each successor $n'$ with priority $g(n') = g(n) + c(n,n')$.
6. If a successor is already in the frontier with a higher cost, replace it with the new cheaper cost.
7. Goto step 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Difference between BFS and UCS:** BFS expands by *number of steps*; UCS expands by *accumulated cost*. When all step costs are **identical (= 1)**, UCS becomes BFS. When step costs vary, UCS is the only correct choice for optimality.

**Real-world engineering utility:** **GPS navigation (OpenStreetMap, Google Maps at city scale)**, **network packet routing (OSPF protocol)**, **airline reservation cheapest-route systems**, **warehouse robot path planning** with energy costs.

---

## 2.3 Depth-First Search (DFS)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Initialize frontier as a LIFO stack containing only $S$.
2. If stack is empty → return **failure**.
3. Pop the **deepest** (newest) node $n$ from the stack.
4. If $n$ is goal → return **success**.
5. Otherwise, expand $n$: push successors onto the stack **in some order** (typically right-to-left so leftmost is on top).
6. Goto step 2.

**The "Why":** Because the stack is LIFO, DFS always explores the **most recently generated node** — i.e., it dives as deep as possible before backtracking.

**Real-world engineering utility:** **Solving mazes and puzzles (Sudoku solvers)**, **topological sorting in build systems (Make, Maven)**, **cycle detection in graphs**, **file-system traversal (Linux `find` command)**, **compiler syntax-tree analysis**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** DFS is **NOT complete** in infinite trees (gets lost forever) and **NOT optimal** (finds the *leftmost* goal, not the *cheapest*). Many students lose marks writing "DFS gives shortest path" — this is FALSE.

---

## 2.4 Depth-Limited Search (DLS)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Same as DFS, but stop expanding a node when its **depth = $\ell$** (the user-supplied limit).
2. Nodes at depth $\ell$ that are not goals are treated as **non-expandable leaves** (cut-off nodes, not failures).
3. Returns three outcomes: **success / failure / cutoff**.

**The "Why":** DFS has a fatal flaw in infinite trees. By imposing a hard depth limit $\ell$, DLS guarantees termination in trees of infinite depth, at the cost of missing goals deeper than $\ell$.

**Real-world engineering utility:** **Playout limits in game-tree search (chess engines)**, **bounded lookahead in autonomous-vehicle planning**, **web crawlers with depth limits** to avoid crawling the entire internet.

---

## 2.5 Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)

**Operational Logic:**
```
for ℓ = 0, 1, 2, 3, ... do
    result ← DLS(problem, ℓ)
    if result = success    then return result
    if result = failure    then return failure
    if result = cutoff     then continue (try next ℓ)
end for
```

**The "Why":** Combines the **completeness of BFS** (with the right $\ell$ sequence) and the **low space of DFS**. Each iteration is a fresh DLS — the stack is cleared between iterations.

**Real-world engineering utility:** **Chess engines (Deep Blue used iterative deepening)**, **package managers solving dependency trees**, **theorem provers**, **planning systems where depth of solution is unknown a-priori**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the single most important table for KTU 2024 — commit it to memory verbatim. Every Part-B question in this module will reference at least one row.**

| Algorithm | Complete? | Optimal? | Time Complexity | Space Complexity |
|-----------|-----------|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **BFS** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (if step cost = 1) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| **UCS** | Yes (if $\varepsilon > 0$) | Yes (always, for finite $\varepsilon$) | $O\!\left(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor}\right)$ | $O\!\left(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor}\right)$ |
| **DFS** | No (fails in infinite trees / loops) | No | $O(b^m)$ | $O(bm)$ |
| **DLS** | No (fails if $\ell < d$) | No | $O(b^\ell)$ | $O(b\ell)$ |
| **IDDFS** | Yes (if $b$ finite) | Yes (if step cost = 1) | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bd)$ |

> **Notation Key (use \vert-style absolute value in answers):** $\vert b \vert$ denotes branching factor, $\vert d \vert$ denotes depth of shallowest goal, $\vert m \vert$ is max depth, $\vert \ell \vert$ is depth limit, $C^*$ is optimal solution cost, $\varepsilon$ is the minimum edge cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic trick for the table:** *"BFS spends, DFS sips."* → BFS = huge memory $O(b^d)$, DFS = tiny memory $O(bm)$. IDDFS = same tiny memory as DFS, but with BFS-level completeness — *best of both worlds*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reference Search Tree (used in ALL derivations below)

Consider the following **uniform-cost binary search tree** with branching factor $b=2$ and depth $= 3$. The goal node is **G**. Edge costs are indicated in parentheses.

$$
\text{Tree structure: } S \xrightarrow{\text{cost}} A \text{ and } B \xrightarrow{\text{cost}} \text{children} \xrightarrow{\text{cost}} \text{grandchildren}
$$

The **search tree expanded by each algorithm** for this problem is shown below in Section 4. Here we derive the **order of node expansion** (which is what KTU asks).

```
                S
               / \
           (1)A   B(2)
             /\   /\
        (1)C  D(1) E(5) F(3)
           |
        (1)G  ← GOAL
```

(Only the left half of a larger tree is fully drawn; B, E, F are children with their own subtrees omitted for clarity.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — BFS Traversal Order on a Sample Tree

Use the **complete binary tree of depth 3** (15 nodes total — easier to demonstrate BFS):

$$
\text{Nodes: } \{S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M, N\}
$$

**BFS Rule:** Expand the *shallowest unexpanded node* (FIFO queue).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

| Step | Action | Frontier (left = next to pop) | Explored |
|------|--------|-------------------------------|----------|
| 0 | Initialize | $[S]$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | Pop $S$ (depth 0), expand | $[A, B]$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | Pop $A$ (depth 1), expand | $[B, C, D]$ | $\{S, A\}$ |
| 3 | Pop $B$ (depth 1), expand | $[C, D, E, F]$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ |
| 4 | Pop $C$ (depth 2), expand | $[D, E, F, G, H]$ | $\{S, A, B, C\}$ |
| 5 | Pop $D$ (depth 2), expand | $[E, F, G, H, I, J]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D\}$ |
| 6 | Pop $E$ (depth 2), expand | $[F, G, H, I, J, K, L]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, E\}$ |
| 7 | Pop $F$ (depth 2), expand | $[G, H, I, J, K, L, M, N]$ | $\{S, A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ |
| 8 | Pop $G$ (depth 3) → **GOAL** | — | — |

**BFS Expansion Order:** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G$ (Goal found at depth 3).

**Key observation:** *All depth-1 nodes are expanded before any depth-2 node, and so on.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — DFS Traversal Order on the Same Tree

**DFS Rule:** Expand the *deepest unexpanded node* (LIFO stack, with children pushed in reverse order so leftmost is on top).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

| Step | Action | Stack (top = next to pop) | Explored |
|------|--------|---------------------------|----------|
| 0 | Initialize | $[S]$ | $\emptyset$ |
| 1 | Pop $S$, push children (B, then A) | $[B, A]$ | $\{S\}$ |
| 2 | Pop $A$ (top), push (D, then C) | $[B, D, C]$ | $\{S, A\}$ |
| 3 | Pop $C$ (top), push (H, then G) | $[B, D, H, G]$ | $\{S, A, C\}$ |
| 4 | Pop $G$ (top) → **GOAL** | — | — |

**DFS Expansion Order:** $S \to A \to C \to G$ (Goal found at depth 3).

**Key observation:** *DFS dives down the leftmost branch to depth 3 immediately, then backtracks if needed.*

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — UCS Traversal Order on a Weighted Graph

**UCS Rule:** Expand node with *lowest path cost $g(n)$* (priority queue).

Use a small weighted graph:

$$
S \xrightarrow{1} A \xrightarrow{3} C \xrightarrow{2} G \quad(\text{Goal}), \quad S \xrightarrow{4} B \xrightarrow{1} D \quad(\text{Goal})
$$

**Path cost table to each node:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(S) &= 0 \\
g(A) &= g(S) + 1 = 1 \\
g(B) &= g(S) + 4 = 4 \\
g(C) &= g(A) + 3 = 1 + 3 = 4 \\
g(D) &= g(B) + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \\
g(G) &= g(C) + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step UCS expansion:**

| Step | Pop (lowest $g$) | Frontier after expansion |
|------|------------------|--------------------------|
| 0 | $S$ (cost 0) | $\{A(1), B(4)\}$ |
| 1 | $A$ (cost 1) | $\{B(4), C(4)\}$ |
| 2 | $B$ (cost 4) *or* $C$ (cost 4) — tie | $\{C(4), D(5)\}$ or $\{B(4), D(5), G(6)\}$ |
| 3 | Continue with lowest | $\ldots$ |
| Final | $G$ (cost 6) | **GOAL** |

**UCS Expansion Order (assuming ties broken alphabetically):** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to G$.

**The crucial point:** UCS finds the goal with **minimum path cost** (here, $g(G)=6$ via $S \to A \to C \to G$). BFS would have stopped at whichever goal is at the *shallowest depth*, not the cheapest cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In a KTU 14-mark question**, you will be given a small graph and asked to list the order of node expansion for ALL FIVE strategies. Always maintain a *priority queue / stack / FIFO queue* explicitly in your answer — this is how the examiner checks correctness.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — DLS and IDDFS on the Same Tree

**DLS with $\ell = 2$ on the 15-node tree:**

Nodes at depth 0, 1, 2 are explored. Goal $G$ at depth 3 is **beyond the limit** → DLS returns **cutoff** (not failure).

**DLS Expansion Order ($\ell=2$):** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F$ (7 nodes expanded, then halt).

**DLS with $\ell = 3$:** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G$ (Goal found).

---

**IDDFS Sequence on the 15-node tree:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration } \ell=0 &: \text{ expand } S. \quad \text{No goal.} \\
\text{Iteration } \ell=1 &: \text{ expand } S, A, B. \quad \text{No goal.} \\
\text{Iteration } \ell=2 &: \text{ expand } S, A, B, C, D, E, F. \quad \text{No goal.} \\
\text{Iteration } \ell=3 &: \text{ expand } S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M, N. \quad \text{Goal at } G.
\end{aligned}
$$

**IDDFS final result:** Goal $G$ found at depth 3.

**Total nodes expanded across all iterations of IDDFS:**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{d} b^k = b^0 + b^1 + b^2 + \cdots + b^d
$$

For $b=2, d=3$:

$$
N_{\text{total}} = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 = 15
$$

(For deeper trees, the lower-depth iterations cost less and the final iteration dominates, giving asymptotic time $O(b^d)$.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IDDFS is the "gold standard" of uninformed search:** It uses only $O(bd)$ memory (like DFS — a stack of depth $d$), but guarantees completeness and optimality (like BFS). It is the **preferred algorithm when the search depth is unknown and memory is tight** — this is exactly what 14-mark KTU questions want you to conclude.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`s1`, `a1`, etc.) to comply with the Mermaid compilation rule. Labels use clean uppercase text only — no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted labels.

---

## 4.2 The Reference Search Tree (used throughout Module 2)

This is the canonical tree for visualising BFS, DFS, DLS, and IDDFS.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[S1 ROOT S]:::root
    a1[A1 LEVEL 1 A]:::l1
    b1[B1 LEVEL 1 B]:::l1
    c1[C1 LEVEL 2 C]:::l2
    d1[D1 LEVEL 2 D]:::l2
    e1[E1 LEVEL 2 E]:::l2
    f1[F1 LEVEL 2 F]:::l2
    g1[G1 LEVEL 3 G GOAL]:::goal
    h1[H1 LEVEL 3 H]:::l3
    i1[I1 LEVEL 3 I]:::l3
    j1[J1 LEVEL 3 J]:::l3
    k1[K1 LEVEL 3 K]:::l3
    l1[L1 LEVEL 3 L]:::l3
    m1[M1 LEVEL 3 M]:::l3
    n1[N1 LEVEL 3 N]:::l3

    s1 --- a1
    s1 --- b1
    a1 --- c1
    a1 --- d1
    b1 --- e1
    b1 --- f1
    c1 --- g1
    c1 --- h1
    d1 --- i1
    d1 --- j1
    e1 --- k1
    e1 --- l1
    f1 --- m1
    f1 --- n1

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef l1 fill:#2e7d8a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef l2 fill:#4a8fbf,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef l3 fill:#8fb8de,stroke:#000
    classDef goal fill:#ffd54f,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

**Legend:** Levels are colour-coded: dark-blue root → teal level-1 → mid-blue level-2 → light-blue level-3 → **gold goal**.

---

## 4.3 BFS Traversal Flow (FIFO Queue Mechanism)

This block diagram shows **how BFS processes nodes level-by-level using a FIFO queue**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[STEP 0 FRONTIER S QUEUE FOLLOWS FIFO RULE]:::init
    P1[STEP 1 POP S EXPAND A AND B]:::step
    P2[STEP 2 POP A DEPTH 1 EXPAND C D]:::step
    P3[STEP 3 POP B DEPTH 1 EXPAND E F]:::step
    P4[STEP 4 POP C DEPTH 2 EXPAND G H]:::step
    P5[STEP 5 POP D DEPTH 2 EXPAND I J]:::step
    P6[STEP 6 POP E DEPTH 2 EXPAND K L]:::step
    P7[STEP 7 POP F DEPTH 2 EXPAND M N]:::step
    P8[STEP 8 POP G DEPTH 3 GOAL FOUND]:::done

    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8

    classDef init fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#4a8fbf,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#ffd54f,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each step pops the *leftmost* (oldest) element of the queue. BFS exhausts **all** depth-$d$ nodes before touching any depth-$(d+1)$ node.

---

## 4.4 DFS Traversal Flow (LIFO Stack Mechanism)

This block diagram shows **how DFS dives deep using a LIFO stack**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q0[STEP 0 STACK S]:::init
    Q1[STEP 1 POP S PUSH B THEN A TOP IS A]:::step
    Q2[STEP 2 POP A PUSH D THEN C TOP IS C]:::step
    Q3[STEP 3 POP C PUSH H THEN G TOP IS G]:::step
    Q4[STEP 4 POP G GOAL FOUND]:::done

    Q0 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4

    classDef init fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#4a8fbf,color:#fff
    classDef done fill:#ffd54f,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each step pops the *topmost* (newest) element of the stack. DFS dives down the leftmost branch as far as possible.

---

## 4.5 IDDFS Outer Loop (the "Telescoping $\ell$" Pattern)

This shows the **IDDFS master control loop** that calls DLS repeatedly with increasing $\ell$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[START]:::init
    L1[ELL EQUALS 0]:::step
    L2[RUN DLS WITH LIMIT ELL]:::step
    L3{WHAT DID DLS RETURN}:::decide
    L4[RETURN SUCCESS PATH]:::done
    L5[RETURN FAILURE NO GOAL EXISTS]:::done
    L6[INCREMENT ELL BY 1 GO TO NEXT ITERATION]:::step

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3
    L3 -- SUCCESS --> L4
    L3 -- FAILURE --> L5
    L3 -- CUTOFF --> L6
    L6 --> L1

    classDef init fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
    classDef step fill:#4a8fbf,color:#fff
    classDef decide fill:#ff9a3c,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef done fill:#ffd54f,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

**Reading the diagram:** IDDFS loops forever (or until memory exhaustion) incrementing $\ell$. The crucial difference from BFS is that **only one branch is in memory at a time** (DFS-style).

---

## 4.6 Comparative Block Architecture — How the Five Strategies Differ Mechanistically

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[UNINFORMED SEARCH ALGORITHMS]:::init
    BFS[BFS FIFO QUEUE LEVEL BY LEVEL COMPLETE YES OPTIMAL YES SPACE O Bd]:::bfs
    UCS[UCS PRIORITY QUEUE BY GN CHEAPEST FIRST COMPLETE YES OPTIMAL YES SPACE O B]:::ucs
    DFS[DFS LIFO STACK DEEPEST FIRST COMPLETE NO OPTIMAL NO SPACE O BM]:::dfs
    DLS[DLS DFS PLUS DEPTH CAP ELL COMPLETE NO OPTIMAL NO SPACE O BELL]:::dls
    IDDFS[IDDFS OUTER LOOP OVER DLS WITH GROWING ELL COMPLETE YES OPTIMAL YES SPACE O BD]:::id

    START --> BFS
    START --> UCS
    START --> DFS
    START --> DLS
    START --> IDDFS
    DLS --> IDDFS

    classDef init fill:#1f3a5f,color:#fff
    classDef bfs fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff
    classDef ucs fill:#1565c0,color:#fff
    classDef dfs fill:#c62828,color:#fff
    classDef dls fill:#ef6c00,color:#fff
    classDef id fill:#6a1b9a,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the colour-coded boxes in the exam:** BFS is *green* (safe and complete), UCS is *blue* (optimal and robust), DFS and DLS are *red/orange* (fast but unsafe), and IDDFS is *purple* (the "best of both worlds" synthesis).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between Informed and Uninformed Search Strategies. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Uninformed Search | Informed Search |
|--------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Use of heuristic $h(n)$ | Not used | Used to guide search |
| Information used | Only $g(n)$ and goal test | $g(n)$ + $h(n)$ |
| Example | BFS, DFS, UCS, DLS, IDDFS | A*, Greedy Best-First Search |
| Efficiency | Lower | Higher (in most cases) |

Uninformed search relies **only on the problem definition** and explores the state space blindly, while informed search employs **domain-specific heuristic knowledge** to estimate the cost from any state to the goal. *(1 mark for clear definition of each + 1 mark for example + 1 mark for distinction table.)*

---

### **Q2. Why is Depth-First Search not complete? Under what condition is it optimal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

DFS is **not complete** because it can get trapped in an **infinite branch** of a state space with infinite depth or cycles, never returning to explore alternative paths that may contain the goal. *(1 mark)*

Even when finite, DFS is **not optimal** because it always explores the *leftmost* (or deepest-first) path; if a shallower goal exists on a different branch, DFS may first find a deeper goal and return it as the solution. *(1 mark)*

DFS becomes **optimal only when** all non-goal nodes have the **same path cost** and the goal is on the branch it explores first — in practice, this is rarely guaranteed. *(1 mark for the condition.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, Full 14-Mark Format]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Breadth-First Search (BFS)** algorithm with a suitable example. Discuss its **completeness, optimality, time complexity, and space complexity**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat example, explain **Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS)**. Show that it combines the **benefits of BFS and DFS**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain **Uniform-Cost Search (UCS)** with a suitable graph example. Show how UCS differs from BFS. Prove that UCS is **optimal when all step costs are non-negative**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Depth-Limited Search (DLS)** and **Iterative Deepening DFS (IDDFS)**. Show the working of both algorithms on the same tree, and discuss the **conditions under which IDDFS is preferred over BFS and DFS**. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.2.1 Model Solution for Question A(a) — BFS Explanation

**BFS Algorithm Definition:** Breadth-First Search is a uniform-cost search in which the cost of each step is identical. BFS expands the **shallowest unexpanded node** first, using a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) queue** as the frontier data structure. *(1 mark)*

**Algorithm Steps:** *(2 marks for the algorithmic description)*

1. Insert the start node $S$ into an empty FIFO queue (frontier) and an empty explored set.
2. If the queue is empty, return **failure** (no goal reachable).
3. Remove the **shallowest** node $n$ from the queue.
4. If $n$ is the goal, return the path from $S$ to $n$ as the solution.
5. Add $n$ to the explored set.
6. Expand $n$: for each successor $n'$ of $n$ that is neither in the frontier nor in the explored set, add $n'$ to the back of the queue.
7. Go to step 2.

**Example (Binary Tree, Goal = G):**

Using the reference tree from Section 3, BFS expands nodes in the order:

$$
S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F \to G
$$

The goal $G$ at depth 3 is found after exploring **all** nodes at depths 0, 1, and 2. *(1 mark for the example expansion.)*

**Complexity Analysis:** *(3 marks for the four properties)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Completeness} &: \text{Yes — BFS is complete if the branching factor } b \text{ is finite.} \\
\text{Optimality} &: \text{Yes — BFS is optimal if all step costs are equal (e.g., cost = 1).} \\
\text{Time Complexity} &: T(b,d) = 1 + b + b^2 + \cdots + b^d = O(b^d) \\
\text{Space Complexity} &: S(b,d) = O(b^d)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical note:** The space requirement $O(b^d)$ is BFS's main weakness — for $b=10, d=12$, this is $10^{12}$ nodes in memory, which is infeasible. This motivates the need for DFS-family algorithms. *(1 mark for the practical remark.)*

---

### 5.2.2 Model Solution for Question A(b) — IDDFS Explanation

**Definition:** Iterative Deepening Depth-First Search (IDDFS) is a **general strategy** that runs DLS with progressively increasing depth limits $\ell = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until the goal is found. It is the **preferred uninformed search method** when the search depth is unknown and memory is constrained. *(1 mark)*

**IDDFS Master Algorithm (pseudocode-style description, no code blocks):** *(2 marks)*

The algorithm is structured as an **outer loop** that increases $\ell$ from $0$ to $\infty$, calling a **modified DLS** as a subroutine at each iteration. If DLS returns *success*, IDDFS returns that path. If DLS returns *cutoff*, the loop increments $\ell$ and tries again. If DLS returns *failure*, IDDFS returns *failure* (the goal is genuinely unreachable).

**Working Example on the Reference Tree:**

For the 15-node binary tree with goal $G$ at depth 3:

- **Iteration $\ell=0$:** Expand only $S$. Frontier: $[S]$. No goal at depth 0. Return cutoff.
- **Iteration $\ell=1$:** Expand $S, A, B$ (one level deeper). No goal. Return cutoff.
- **Iteration $\ell=2$:** Expand $S, A, B, C, D, E, F$ (depths 0, 1, 2). No goal. Return cutoff.
- **Iteration $\ell=3$:** Expand $S, A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H, I, J, K, L, M, N$. Goal $G$ found! Return success. *(2 marks for the iterative example.)*

**Why IDDFS Combines BFS and DFS:** *(2 marks)*

| Property | BFS | DFS | IDDFS |
|----------|-----|-----|-------|
| Complete | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Optimal (unit costs) | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Time | $O(b^d)$ | $O(b^m)$ | $O(b^d)$ |
| Space | $O(b^d)$ | $O(bm)$ | $O(bd)$ |

**Conclusion:** IDDFS inherits BFS's **completeness and optimality** (because each depth level is fully explored in order), while inheriting DFS's **low memory footprint** $O(bd)$ (only one branch of the tree is in memory at a time). The price paid is **re-expansion of nodes at shallower depths** across iterations — but for large $b$ and $d$, this overhead is asymptotically negligible because the deepest iteration dominates the total work.

**Total nodes expanded by IDDFS:**

$$
N_{\text{IDDFS}} = (d+1) \cdot 1 + d \cdot b + (d-1) \cdot b^2 + \cdots + 1 \cdot b^d = O(b^d)
$$

The factor $(d+1)$ compared to BFS's single $b^d$ gives a small constant overhead. *(1 mark for the analysis.)*

---

### 5.2.3 Model Solution for Question B(a) — UCS Explanation

**UCS Definition:** Uniform-Cost Search expands the node $n$ with the **lowest path cost $g(n)$** in the frontier. It is essentially **Dijkstra's algorithm** applied to a state-space search problem, and uses a **priority queue** (min-heap) keyed on $g(n)$. *(1 mark)*

**UCS Algorithm (textual description):** *(1 mark)*

1. Insert the start node $S$ into the priority queue with priority $g(S)=0$.
2. If the priority queue is empty, return **failure**.
3. Pop the node $n$ with **minimum $g(n)$**.
4. If $n$ is the goal, return the solution path.
5. For each successor $n'$ of $n$ with step cost $c(n, n') \geq 0$:
   - Compute $g(n') = g(n) + c(n, n')$.
   - If $n'$ is not in the frontier or explored set, insert $n'$ with priority $g(n')$.
   - If $n'$ is already in the frontier with a higher $g$, replace the old entry with the lower cost.

**Example (Weighted Graph):**

Consider the graph: $S \to A$ (cost 1), $S \to B$ (cost 4), $A \to C$ (cost 3), $B \to D$ (cost 1), $C \to G$ (cost 2, goal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(S) &= 0 \\
g(A) &= 0 + 1 = 1 \\
g(B) &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
g(C) &= 1 + 3 = 4 \\
g(D) &= 4 + 1 = 5 \\
g(G) &= 4 + 2 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**UCS Order:** $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to G$. The path to $G$ is $S \to A \to C \to G$ with cost $6$. *(1 mark for the example.)*

**Proof of Optimality of UCS:** *(3 marks)*

*Claim:* UCS returns an optimal goal (minimum-cost path) when all step costs are non-negative.

*Proof Sketch:* Suppose UCS terminates by popping a goal node $n_{\text{goal}}$ from the priority queue. Every other node $n'$ in the frontier has $g(n') \geq g(n_{\text{goal}})$ because UCS always pops the minimum. If there existed a cheaper goal $n_{\text{goal}}'$ with $g(n_{\text{goal}}') < g(n_{\text{goal}})$, then $n_{\text{goal}}'$ would have been in the frontier (or already explored) earlier than $n_{\text{goal}}$, contradicting the popping order. Hence $g(n_{\text{goal}})$ must be the minimum. $\blacksquare$

*Prerequisite:* The condition $c(n, n') \geq 0$ for all edges ensures that path costs along any route are **monotonically non-decreasing** — this is what makes Dijkstra-style expansion correct.

**Comparison with BFS:** *(1 mark for the distinction)*

| Aspect | BFS | UCS |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Order of expansion | Shallowest first | Lowest $g(n)$ first |
| Optimal for unit step costs? | Yes | Yes (special case) |
| Optimal for varying step costs? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Frontier data structure | FIFO queue | Priority queue |

---

### 5.2.4 Model Solution for Question B(b) — DLS and IDDFS Detailed

**DLS Definition:** Depth-Limited Search is DFS with a **predetermined depth limit $\ell$**. Nodes at depth $\ell$ are not expanded further. DLS returns one of three outcomes: **success** (goal found within the limit), **failure** (the entire tree is finite and no goal exists), or **cutoff** (no goal found within the limit, but unexplored nodes exist beyond $\ell$). *(1 mark)*

**DLS Example on the 15-node tree with $\ell = 2$:** *(1 mark)*

DLS expands: $S \to A \to B \to C \to D \to E \to F$ (all nodes at depths 0, 1, 2). Goal $G$ at depth 3 is not reached. DLS returns **cutoff**.

**IDDFS (recall from Question A(b)):** Iteratively applies DLS with $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ until success or genuine failure. The earlier iterative example on the same tree produces the goal at iteration $\ell = 3$. *(1 mark for repeating the working.)*

**Conditions Where IDDFS is Preferred:** *(4 marks for the comparative discussion)*

1. **When search depth $d$ is unknown a-priori** — BFS would explore infinite levels if $d$ is not bounded; DFS might miss the goal in the "wrong" branch; IDDFS adapts automatically.
2. **When memory is severely constrained** — IDDFS uses only $O(bd)$ space, like DFS, while BFS needs $O(b^d)$ — often infeasible for large state spaces.
3. **When the branching factor $b$ is large** — IDDFS's overhead of re-expanding shallower nodes is a small constant factor; the deepest iteration $O(b^d)$ dominates the work.
4. **When step costs are uniform (unit cost)** — IDDFS is optimal and complete under this condition, matching BFS in correctness with far less memory.
5. **Classic use cases** — chess engines (Deep Blue, Stockfish), 15-puzzle solvers, Rubik's cube solvers, general game-playing engines.

**Conclusion:** IDDFS is the **recommended default uninformed search** when the depth of the solution is not known in advance and memory is the primary bottleneck.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks in Module 2 (Uninformed Search):**
>
> 1. **Confusing BFS and UCS:** BFS expands by *depth*; UCS expands by *path cost*. Students often write "BFS finds the shortest path" — this is true ONLY when all step costs are equal. UCS is the **generalized version** for arbitrary non-negative costs.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mark DFS as incomplete:** Writing "DFS is complete" is a guaranteed **−2 mark** penalty. Always state that DFS is incomplete in infinite state spaces or in graphs with cycles (without a visited set).
>
> 3. **Wrong frontier data structure:** BFS uses **FIFO queue**, DFS uses **LIFO stack**, UCS uses **priority queue (min-heap)**. Mixing them up will lose 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not defining variables $b, d, m, \ell$ before using them:** In the complexity table, examiners look for variable definitions. Always begin with "Let $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal..."
>
> 5. **IDDFS analysis missing re-expansion count:** The total work is $O(b^d)$, but the leading constant is slightly higher than BFS. Students often forget to mention that IDDFS re-generates nodes at depths $0, 1, \ldots, d-1$ in successive iterations — this is the *trade-off* the examiner wants to see.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Uninformed Search (KTU Module 2)**

- **Uninformed Search** = No heuristic $h(n)$ used. Only $g(n)$ and goal test.
- **BFS** = FIFO queue, expands shallowest, **complete and optimal (unit costs)**, time/space = $O(b^d)$.
- **UCS** = Priority queue by $g(n)$, **complete and optimal (any non-negative step costs)**, time/space = $O(b^{1+\lfloor C^*/\varepsilon \rfloor})$.
- **DFS** = LIFO stack, expands deepest, **NOT complete (infinite trees), NOT optimal**, time = $O(b^m)$, space = $O(bm)$.
- **DLS** = DFS with depth cap $\ell$, returns *success / failure / cutoff*, time = $O(b^\ell)$, space = $O(b\ell)$.
- **IDDFS** = Outer loop over DLS with $\ell = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ until goal found. **Complete and optimal**, time = $O(b^d)$, space = $O(bd)$.
- **Key Variables:** $b$ = branching factor, $d$ = depth of shallowest goal, $m$ = max depth, $\ell$ = depth limit, $C^*$ = optimal solution cost, $\varepsilon$ = min step cost.
- **Mnemonic:** *"BFS spends, DFS sips, IDDFS is the sweet spot."*
- **Default Recommendation:** If you do not know the depth of the solution and memory is tight → use **IDDFS**.
- **Exam-Specific:** Always show the **frontier and explored set** explicitly in step-by-step expansions. Always state **complete / optimal** properties for each algorithm. Always define variables in complexity formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heuristic Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heuristic Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Informed Search Algorithms** within Artificial Intelligence, a **Heuristic Function**, conventionally denoted as **$h(n)$**, is a **problem-specific estimation function** that estimates the cost of the cheapest (optimal) path from any given state $n$ to a designated **goal state**. It acts as a domain-knowledge-driven "rule of thumb" that guides the search process toward the most promising frontier nodes, thereby drastically reducing the search space explored.

Formally, for a state $n$ in the state space:

$$h(n) = \text{estimated cost from } n \text{ to the nearest goal state}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A heuristic function $h(n)$ is a function that takes a state $n$ as input and returns a non-negative number which is an estimate of the cost of the cheapest path from $n$ to a goal state. It is the **"informed"** component that distinguishes Informed Search from Uninformed Search (BFS, DFS, UCS).

### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are driving from **Kochi to Delhi** without a GPS, but you have a *paper map*.

*   **Uninformed Search (e.g., BFS):** You blindly check every single road junction radiating outward until you accidentally hit Delhi. This takes forever.
*   **Heuristic Function ($h(n)$):** You use a **straight-line ruler on the paper map** to measure the "as the crow flies" distance from your current city to Delhi. Although roads are not straight, this estimate tells you that moving **North** is far more promising than moving **South** or **East**. This estimate is your heuristic.

The straight-line distance is **never perfectly accurate** (it ignores roads, mountains, rivers), but it is **computationally cheap** and **directionally useful**. This is precisely the role of $h(n)$ in AI search.

### 1.3 Terminology & Key Metrics

*   **$h^*(n)$** — The **true (actual) minimum cost** from state $n$ to the goal. This is generally unknown and what $h(n)$ is trying to estimate.
*   **$g(n)$** — The cost of the path so far from the start state to state $n$ (used in A*).
*   **$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$** — The estimated total cost of a solution through $n$ (the core of A* Search).
*   **Informedness** — A heuristic is *more informed* if it produces an estimate closer to the true cost $h^*(n)$ without exceeding it.
*   **Admissible Heuristic** — Bold term: a heuristic that **never overestimates** the true cost: $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$.
*   **Consistent (Monotone) Heuristic** — Bold term: a heuristic that satisfies the triangle inequality: $h(n) \leq \text{StepCost}(n, n') + h(n')$.

> [!NOTE]
> The heuristic function is the **single most important design choice** in Informed Search. A well-designed heuristic can reduce exponential search to polynomial search, while a poor one can degrade A* into a brute-force search.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — How $h(n)$ Works

The heuristic function operates on a **node-by-node basis** during the search frontier expansion. The logic proceeds as follows:

1.  **State Encoding:** Every node $n$ in the search tree represents a complete configuration of the world (e.g., a board position, a city map, a variable assignment).
2.  **Domain Knowledge Injection:** $h(n)$ uses pre-computed, human-encoded knowledge about the *structure* of the problem to assign a numeric "promise" score to each node.
3.  **Frontier Sorting:** The priority queue (open list) of the search algorithm is sorted by $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Nodes with **lower $h(n)$** are deemed closer to the goal and are expanded first.
4.  **Pruning Effect:** By systematically expanding the "most promising" node first, the algorithm avoids expanding vast subtrees that heuristically look unfavorable.

### 2.2 Canonical Properties of a Heuristic Function

For KTU examinations, three properties are **guaranteed high-yield**:

**Property A — Admissibility (Optimism Condition)**

A heuristic is **admissible** if it never overestimates the true cost to reach the goal. Mathematically:

$$h(n) \leq h^*(n) \quad \forall \, n$$

*Engineering Utility:* An admissible heuristic guarantees that A* will return an **optimal solution** (with non-negative step costs). This is a non-negotiable property for safety-critical AI (e.g., robotics path planning, medical diagnosis).

**Property B — Consistency / Monotonicity (Triangle Inequality)**

A heuristic is **consistent** if, for every node $n$ and every successor $n'$ generated by action $a$:

$$h(n) \leq c(n, a, n') + h(n')$$

*Engineering Utility:* A consistent heuristic ensures that the $f$-values along any path are **non-decreasing**, which guarantees that the first time a node is expanded in A*, the optimal path to it has been found. This eliminates the need for costly re-expansions.

**Property C — Dominance**

Heuristic $h_a$ **dominates** $h_b$ if:

$$h_b(n) \leq h_a(n) \quad \forall \, n \quad \text{(and both are admissible)}$$

The dominating heuristic expands **fewer or equal nodes** and is always preferred in practice.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a high-density reference for KTU 2024 exam solutions.

| **Symbol / Property** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning** | **KTU Exam Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heuristic Estimate | $h(n)$ | Estimated cost from $n$ to goal | Defining term (2 marks) |
| True Optimal Cost | $h^*(n)$ | Actual minimum cost from $n$ to goal | Used in admissibility proof |
| A* Evaluation Function | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Estimated total path cost via $n$ | Core of A* algorithm (7 marks) |
| Admissibility | $h(n) \leq h^*(n)$ | Never overestimates | Guarantees A* optimality |
| Consistency | $h(n) \leq c(n,n') + h(n')$ | Triangle inequality | Guarantees no re-expansions |
| Dominance | $h_a(n) \geq h_b(n)$ (both admissible) | $h_a$ is "more informed" | Reduces search space |
| Path Max Equation | $C^* = f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | Cost of optimal solution | Used in termination |
| 8-Puzzle: Misplaced Tiles | $h_1(n) = \text{count of non-goal tiles}$ | Counts misplaced tiles | Frequent 7-mark problem |
| 8-Puzzle: Manhattan Distance | $h_2(n) = \sum \vert x_i^{cur} - x_i^{goal} \vert + \vert y_i^{cur} - y_i^{goal} \vert$ | Sum of grid distances per tile | Frequent 7-mark problem |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heuristic functions are the backbone of modern AI systems. Notable applications include:

*   **Google Maps / GPS Navigation:** Uses straight-line distance heuristic in A* to find the shortest driving route.
*   **Game AI (Chess, Go):** The evaluation function $h(n)$ estimates the "goodness" of a board position.
*   **Robotics Motion Planning:** Heuristics guide robot arms and autonomous vehicles through obstacle-laden environments.
*   **Automated Theorem Proving:** Heuristics guide the search for proofs in formal logic systems.
*   **Logistics & Supply Chain:** The Traveling Salesman Problem uses heuristics (e.g., nearest neighbor) to find near-optimal delivery routes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example: 8-Puzzle Heuristic Computation

The 8-puzzle is the **most frequently asked problem** in KTU exams on heuristic functions. We will compute both $h_1$ and $h_2$ for a specific configuration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** Consider the following 8-puzzle. Compute (a) the Misplaced Tiles heuristic $h_1(n)$ and (b) the Manhattan Distance heuristic $h_2(n)$.

**Current State ($n$):**

$$
\begin{matrix}
2 & 8 & 3 \\
1 & 6 & 4 \\
7 & \square & 5
\end{matrix}
$$

**Goal State:**

$$
\begin{matrix}
1 & 2 & 3 \\
8 & \square & 4 \\
7 & 6 & 5
\end{matrix}
$$

---

#### Part (a) — Misplaced Tiles Heuristic $h_1(n)$

**Step 1:** Define the heuristic formally.

$$h_1(n) = \text{Number of tiles that are NOT in their goal position}$$

The blank tile ($\square$) is **excluded** from the count.

**Step 2:** Compare each tile position with its goal position.

| **Tile** | **Current Position** | **Goal Position** | **Match?** | **Contributes to $h_1$?** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | (Row 2, Col 1) | (Row 1, Col 1) | ✗ Mismatch | Yes |
| 2 | (Row 1, Col 1) | (Row 1, Col 2) | ✗ Mismatch | Yes |
| 3 | (Row 1, Col 3) | (Row 1, Col 3) | ✓ Match | No |
| 4 | (Row 2, Col 3) | (Row 2, Col 3) | ✓ Match | No |
| 5 | (Row 3, Col 3) | (Row 3, Col 3) | ✓ Match | No |
| 6 | (Row 3, Col 2) | (Row 3, Col 2) | ✓ Match | No |
| 7 | (Row 3, Col 1) | (Row 3, Col 1) | ✓ Match | No |
| 8 | (Row 1, Col 2) | (Row 2, Col 1) | ✗ Mismatch | Yes |

**Step 3:** Count the mismatches.

$$h_1(n) = 3$$
