## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]`

> **Define adversarial search. Why is it more complex than standard uninformed/informed search? Mention the four defining properties of a game as per the KTU AI syllabus.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Adversarial Search** is the search technique used when the agent's environment contains an *opponent* with conflicting goals, requiring the agent to plan under worst-case opposition.

It is more complex than standard search because:
1. The search tree models an **active adversary**, not just a passive state space.
2. Solution is a **strategy (contingency plan)**, not merely a path.
3. Time horizon is often limited by *move clock*, not by goal reachability.
4. Branching factors in games (e.g., 35 in chess) are typically much larger than in route-finding.

**Four properties of a game (KTU standard):**
1. **Initial state** $S_0$
2. **Successor function** specifying legal moves
3. **Terminal test** identifying end of game
4. **Utility function** assigning numeric payoff at terminal states

*(Examiner's note: Students often miss the 4th property — utility is part of the formal definition, not optional.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

> **List and briefly explain any three key differences between the MinMax algorithm and the Alpha-Beta pruning algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | MinMax | Alpha-Beta |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Compute the optimal value at the root | Same — but with fewer node visits |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(b^d)$ — always | $O(b^{d/2})$ — best case (perfect ordering) |
| **Auxiliary Information** | None | Maintains $\alpha$ and $\beta$ bounds at each recursive call |
| **Result** | Optimal action | Optimal action (identical) |
| **Worst Case** | Examines all $b^d$ leaves | Can degenerate to $O(b^d)$ with adversarial move ordering |
| **Optimal Ordering** | Irrelevant | Can halve the effective branching factor |

*(Examiner's note: A common mistake is to claim Alpha-Beta changes the answer — it does NOT; it only prunes branches that provably cannot affect the final decision.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split across two sub-parts, typically **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Internal choice is **mandatory** per KTU 2024 norms — the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Explain the MinMax algorithm in detail. Show the step-by-step computation of backed-up values for the following game tree and determine the optimal first move for MAX. Terminal leaf values (left to right) are: 3, 12, 2, 15, 8, 10.**

```
                              (Root) MAX
                             /          \
                       A (MIN)            B (MIN)
                       /   \               /    \
                  3   12  2           15   8  10
```

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**  
The MinMax algorithm computes the optimal strategy for MAX in a two-player, zero-sum, deterministic, perfect-information game. The root value is computed bottom-up by alternating $\max$ (at MAX nodes) and $\min$ (at MIN nodes) operations.

**Step 2 — Initialize leaf values (1 mark):**  
Leaves are given: $3, 12, 2$ are the children of A; $15, 8, 10$ are the children of B.

**Step 3 — Compute Left MAX subtree value (1.5 marks):**  
The three leaves belong to the MAX-nodes directly above them (in this tree, the second level). Compute:
$$\text{MAX}_{\text{left group}} = \max(3, 12, 2) = 12$$

**Step 4 — Compute Right MAX subtree value (1.5 marks):**  
$$\text{MAX}_{\text{right group}} = \max(15, 8, 10) = 15$$

**Step 5 — Propagate to MIN nodes (1 mark):**  
MIN chooses the *smaller* of the values from its children. Here, each MIN node has just one group child each:
- Left MIN (A) receives $12$ from its MAX-descendant.
- Right MIN (B) receives $15$ from its MAX-descendant.

**Step 6 — Final root decision (1 mark):**  
The root MAX chooses:
$$v(\text{root}) = \max(12, 15) = 15$$

**Optimal action:** The agent should move toward the **Right** subtree (B), guaranteeing utility $15$ against optimal play.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **What is Alpha-Beta Pruning? Apply Alpha-Beta Pruning to the game tree below and identify all pruned nodes. Terminal values (left to right): 3, 5, 2, 8.**

```
                          (Root) MAX
                         /          \
                   A (MIN)            B (MIN)
                   /    \             /    \
                  3     5           2      8
```

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Alpha-Beta Pruning (1.5 marks):**  
Alpha-Beta Pruning is an optimization of MinMax that eliminates branches that *cannot possibly influence the final decision*. It maintains two parameters:
- $\alpha$ = the value of the best (highest) MAX option found on the current path.
- $\beta$ = the value of the best (lowest) MIN option found on the current path.

**Pruning condition:** Stop exploring a branch when $\alpha \geq \beta$.

**Step 2 — Initialization (0.5 mark):**  
At root: $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 3 — Explore Left Subtree A (MIN) (1.5 marks):**  
- Examine leaf $3$. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 3) = 3$. Check: $\alpha = -\infty < \beta = 3$ → continue.
- Examine leaf $5$. Update $\beta = \min(3, 5) = 3$. No change. A's value $= 3$.

**Step 4 — Back at Root, update $\alpha$ (1 mark):**  
$\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$. Bounds: $\alpha = 3$, $\beta = +\infty$.

**Step 5 — Explore Right Subtree B (MIN) (1.5 marks):**  
- Examine leaf $2$. Update $\beta = \min(+\infty, 2) = 2$. Check: $\alpha = 3 \geq \beta = 2$ → **PRUNE!**
- Leaf $8$ is never examined.

**Step 6 — Final decision (1 mark):**  
$v(\text{root}) = \max(v(A), v(B)) = \max(3, 2) = 3$.  
**Optimal action: Left (toward A).**  
**Pruned node: the right child of B (leaf value 8).**

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> **Explain in detail the four key components of a game in AI. With the help of a suitable example, show how optimal decisions are made in a game using the concept of a game tree.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Four Components of a Game (3 marks):**

1. **Initial State $S_0$:** Specifies how the game starts. *Example:* Empty $3 \times 3$ board in Tic-Tac-Toe.
2. **Successor Function $S(s)$:** A set of legal moves from any state $s$. *Example:* In Tic-Tac-Toe, the moves are all empty cells.
3. **Terminal Test:** A predicate that determines if the game is over. *Example:* A win, loss, or full board.
4. **Utility Function $U(p)$:** Numeric payoff for player $p$ at terminal states. *Example:* $+1$ for X-win, $-1$ for O-win, $0$ for draw.

**Step 2 — Game Tree Construction (2 marks):**  
Each node is a state. Branches are moves. The tree depth is the number of *plies* (player turns). For a deterministic, perfect-information, two-player game, the tree is fully specified by the four components above.

**Step 3 — Optimal Decision via MinMax (2 marks):**  
The agent at the root assumes the opponent plays *optimally against it*. The agent chooses the move that maximizes the *guaranteed* utility — i.e., the backed-up MinMax value at the root. The optimal decision is *contingent* on the opponent's replies at every level, making it a **strategy** rather than a single path.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> **Discuss the time and space complexity of the MinMax algorithm. With a clearly worked example, demonstrate how Alpha-Beta Pruning reduces the number of nodes explored. What is the effective branching factor in the best case?**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — MinMax Complexity (1.5 marks):**  
- **Time:** $O(b^d)$ — must visit every node in the tree of depth $d$ and branching factor $b$.
- **Space:** $O(bd)$ — depth-first implementation needs to store only the path.

**Step 2 — Worked Example of Alpha-Beta (3 marks):**  
Consider the tree:
```
                          (Root) MAX
                         /          \
                   A (MIN)            B (MIN)
                   /    \             /    \
                  3     5           2      8
```
Initial $\alpha = -\infty$, $\beta = +\infty$.

- Explore A's left leaf (3): $\beta = 3$. No prune.
- Explore A's right leaf (5): $\beta = \min(3, 5) = 3$. A returns $3$.
- At root, $\alpha = \max(-\infty, 3) = 3$.
- Explore B's left leaf (2): $\beta = 2$. Check: $\alpha = 3 \geq \beta = 2$ → **PRUNE the right leaf (8)**.
- Root decision: $v = \max(3, 2) = 3$, optimal action = Left.

**Nodes examined: 3 out of 4 leaves (saving 1 leaf).**  
Plain MinMax would have examined all 4.

**Step 3 — Effective Branching Factor (1.5 marks):**  
In the **best case** (perfect move ordering), Alpha-Beta explores approximately $b^{d/2}$ leaves. Comparing with MinMax's $b^d$, the effective branching factor becomes:
$$b_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{b}$$
and the effective depth searched for the *same time budget* is **doubled** to $2d$.

**Step 4 — Conditions for Best-Case Pruning (1 mark):**  
- The best move at each MAX node must be examined *first*.
- The worst move at each MIN node must be examined *first*.
- Achieved in practice via iterative deepening and transposition tables.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "Optimal Decision" with "Best Path":** A MinMax optimal decision is a *strategy tree* (contingency plan), NOT a single path. Marks are deducted if the student treats the solution as just one branch.
> 2. **Wrong Pruning Direction:** Some students prune when $\alpha \leq \beta$ instead of $\alpha \geq \beta$. Memorize: "When the lower bound (alpha) meets the upper bound (beta), cut."
> 3. **Forgetting to Update Bounds After Each Leaf:** Alpha and beta must be updated *after every child evaluation*, not just at the start of recursion.
> 4. **Omitting the Initialization ($\alpha = -\infty, \beta = +\infty$):** Examiners specifically check this. Always start with these.
> 5. **Treating Alpha-Beta as a New Algorithm:** It is *the same* MinMax algorithm with pruning — the *result* is identical to MinMax.
> 6. **Mis-stating Complexity:** Writing "Alpha-Beta is $O(b^d)$" without qualifying *best/worst case* loses 1 mark. Always specify: "Best case $O(b^{d/2})$, worst case $O(b^d)$."

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Game:** Formal model of strategic interaction with 4 components (initial state, successor function, terminal test, utility).
- **Adversarial Search:** Search under opposition; solution is a *strategy*, not a path.
- **Zero-Sum Game:** MAX's gain = MIN's loss; $U_{MAX} = -U_{MIN}$.
- **MinMax Value:** Backed-up value at the root assuming rational opposition.
- **Optimal Decision:** Move that maximizes the *guaranteed* (worst-case) utility.

### 🔑 Algorithm Mechanics
- **MAX nodes:** at even depth, take the *maximum* of children.
- **MIN nodes:** at odd depth, take the *minimum* of children.
- **$\alpha$ (alpha):** Lower bound on MAX's outcome; *initially* $-\infty$.
- **$\beta$ (beta):** Upper bound on MIN's outcome; *initially* $+\infty$.
- **Pruning Rule:** Cut off the branch when $\alpha \geq \beta$.

### 🔑 Complexity & Efficiency
- **MinMax Time:** $O(b^d)$; **Space:** $O(bd)$.
- **Alpha-Beta Time (best case):** $O(b^{d/2})$; effective branching factor $\sqrt{b}$.
- **Alpha-Beta Time (worst case):** $O(b^d)$ (no pruning).
- **Effective depth searched (best case):** $2d$ (looks twice as deep).

### 🔑 Exam Heuristics
- Always **draw the tree** clearly with MAX/MIN labels before computing.
- Always **show the final backed-up value at the root** in bold or boxed.
- Always **state the optimal action** explicitly in words.
- Always **list pruned nodes** when explaining Alpha-Beta.
- For complexity questions, **specify best vs. worst case**.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Treating Alpha-Beta as a *different* algorithm → ✅ It is MinMax + pruning.
- ❌ Writing the wrong pruning condition ($\alpha \leq \beta$) → ✅ Correct: $\alpha \geq \beta$.
- ❌ Forgetting to update $\alpha$ at MAX nodes and $\beta$ at MIN nodes.
- ❌ Confusing "depth" with "ply" — one *ply* is one player's move; one *move* is two plies.
- ❌ Ignoring move-ordering when discussing real-world efficiency.

### 🔑 One-Line Final Summary
> **MinMax computes the optimal guaranteed value by recursively applying $\max$ and $\min$ at alternate tree levels; Alpha-Beta Pruning preserves this exact answer while skipping branches where $\alpha \geq \beta$, achieving $O(b^{d/2})$ time in the best case.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint Satisfaction Problems - Constraint Propagation, Inferences in CSPs, Backtracking Search for CSPs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Constraint Satisfaction Problems - Constraint Propagation, Inferences in CSPs, Backtracking Search for CSPs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)

A **Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP)** is defined formally by a triple $(X, D, C)$ where:

- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ is a finite set of **variables**.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ is a set of **domains**, where $D_i$ is the finite set of possible values for variable $X_i$.
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ is a set of **constraints**. Each constraint $C_k$ is a pair $\langle \text{scope}, \text{relation} \rangle$, where $\text{scope}$ is a tuple of variables that participate in the constraint and $\text{relation}$ defines the allowed combinations of values for those variables.

A **solution** to a CSP is an assignment of values to all variables such that every constraint is satisfied simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A CSP differs from a generic state-space search because the state is represented by a structured set of variables with explicit constraints, allowing the use of *constraint propagation* techniques that exploit the structure of the problem.

### 1.2 Intuition: The Wedding Seating Analogy

Imagine you are arranging **5 guests (variables)** at a round dinner table, where each guest has a list of **food preferences (domain)**. The **constraints** are rules like "Alice cannot sit next to Bob" or "Charlie must sit next to the vegetarian dish." A CSP is precisely this situation, but expressed mathematically so that an algorithm can systematically find an assignment (a seating plan) that obeys every rule.

Think of it this way:
- **Variables** = Chairs to fill.
- **Domains** = Possible guests who can sit in each chair.
- **Constraints** = Rules about who can sit near whom.
- **Constraint Propagation** = Realising that "Alice hates Bob, Bob is the only option for Chair 2, so Alice cannot be in Chair 1 or Chair 3" — eliminating impossible choices *before* you even start placing people.
- **Backtracking Search** = Trying a guest in Chair 1, then Chair 2, and if you get stuck at Chair 3, *going back* to Chair 2 and trying the next possible guest.

### 1.3 Constraint Propagation

**Constraint Propagation** is the process of using the constraints to *reduce* the number of legal values for variables, i.e., to shrink their domains. It is a form of **inference** — deducing new facts from existing constraints without actually searching.

The most common form is **Arc Consistency (AC)**:
- A variable $X_i$ is **arc-consistent** with respect to $X_j$ if for every value $d \in D_i$, there exists some value $e \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied by the pair $(d, e)$.
- A CSP is **arc-consistent** if every variable is arc-consistent with every other variable that it is constrained with.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Arc consistency does **not** solve the CSP. It only simplifies the problem by removing values that can never participate in any solution. The CSP may still require search afterwards.

### 1.4 Inferences in CSPs

Inferences are deductions made by the solver during search. The standard inferences used in CSPs are:

1. **Node Consistency** — Removing values from a unary-constrained variable's domain.
2. **Arc Consistency** — Ensuring every value in a variable's domain has a supporting value in the neighbour's domain.
3. **Path Consistency** — Stronger than arc consistency; considers triples of variables.
4. **Variable Elimination / Forward Checking** — When a variable is assigned, prune inconsistent values from its unassigned neighbours.

### 1.5 Backtracking Search for CSPs

**Backtracking Search** is a depth-first search that:
1. Assigns values to variables **one at a time**.
2. Checks constraints against the current partial assignment only (this is called **constraint checking**).
3. If a dead-end is reached (no legal value for the next variable), it **backtracks** to the most recent variable and tries its next value.

The "vanilla" version is inefficient; in KTU exams, you are expected to know the augmentations: **MRV (Minimum Remaining Values)**, **LCV (Least Constraining Value)**, **Forward Checking**, and **AC-3 inference during search**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Domain Standard:** Throughout KTU 2024 Scheme, CSP notation follows Russell & Norvig's standard text (*Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach*). Domains are finite unless explicitly noted as continuous.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constraint Graph

A binary CSP (where every constraint involves at most 2 variables) is represented as a **constraint graph**:
- **Nodes** = Variables $X_i$.
- **Edges** = Binary constraints between variables.

### 2.2 Arc Consistency: Formal Definition

A **directed arc** $(X_i, X_j)$ is **arc-consistent** if for every value $x \in D_i$ there exists some value $y \in D_j$ such that $(x, y)$ satisfies the binary constraint $C(X_i, X_j)$.

The **Make-Arc-Consistent** primitive operation REVISE$(X_i, X_j)$:

$$\text{REVISE}(X_i, X_j) = \begin{cases} \text{Remove } x \text{ from } D_i \text{ if} \\ \exists\, y \in D_j : (x, y) \text{ satisfies } C(X_i, X_j) \end{cases}$$

A value $x \in D_i$ that has **no supporting value** in $D_j$ is removed because it can never participate in any consistent assignment with $X_j$.

### 2.3 The AC-3 Algorithm

**AC-3** is the canonical arc consistency algorithm. It repeatedly REVISE arcs until no domain changes.

**Pseudocode logic (KTU-exam style):**

```
function AC-3(csp):
    queue ← all arcs (X_i, X_j) in csp
    while queue is not empty:
        (X_i, X_j) ← POP(queue)
        if REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
            if D_i becomes empty: return FAILURE
            for each X_k in NEIGHBOURS[X_i] − {X_j}:
                add (X_k, X_i) to queue
    return csp
```

**Complexity of AC-3:** For a CSP with $n$ variables, each domain of size at most $d$, and $e$ binary constraints:

$$O(e \cdot d^3)$$

This is because a queue entry can be added only $d$ times (each time one value is removed from a domain), and checking REVISE costs $O(d^2)$.

### 2.4 Heuristics for Backtracking Search

| **Heuristic** | **Symbol / Idea** | **Why It Helps** |
|---|---|---|
| **MRV** — Minimum Remaining Values | Pick variable with smallest domain | Fails fast on dead-ends (fail-first principle) |
| **Degree Heuristic** | Pick variable involved in most constraints on remaining vars | Reduces branching factor on heavily-constrained vars |
| **LCV** — Least Constraining Value | Pick value that rules out fewest choices for neighbours | Maximises flexibility for remaining variables |
| **Forward Checking** | When $X_i$ is assigned, prune $D_j$ for each unassigned neighbour | Detects failures early |
| **Arc Consistency (AC-3) During Search** | Run AC-3 after each assignment | Strongest inference; most pruning |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Complexity / Note** |
|---|---|---|
| CSP triple | $(X, D, C)$ | $X$ = vars, $D$ = domains, $C$ = constraints |
| Arc consistency condition | $\forall x \in D_i,\; \exists y \in D_j : (x,y) \in C$ | Removes unsupported values |
| AC-3 worst-case | $O(e \cdot d^3)$ | $n$ vars, $\vert D_i \vert \le d$, $e$ binary arcs |
| Backtracking (basic) worst-case | $O(d^n)$ | Exponential without inference |
| Forward checking cost per step | $O(d^2 \cdot k)$ where $k$ is unassigned neighbours |  |
| Tightness of constraint | Ratio of disallowed tuples to total tuples in relation | High tightness $\Rightarrow$ easy to prune |
| Tree-structured CSP | Solvable in $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | No backtracking needed after arc consistency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid the pipe symbol error:** In the table above, $\vert D_i \vert$ uses `\vert` instead of `|` so that markdown tables do not break. Always write `\|` or `\vert` for absolute value inside tables.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

CSPs are foundational in:
- **Scheduling** (timetabling, airline crew rostering).
- **Configuration problems** (configuring a car, computer, or industrial system with thousands of options and constraints).
- **Map colouring** (graph colouring for register allocation in compilers, frequency assignment in mobile networks).
- **Cryptarithmetic puzzles** (SEND + MORE = MONEY) — a classic KTU exam example.
- **Sudoku, N-Queens, Crossword puzzles**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Arc Consistency on a Map-Colouring CSP

**Problem:** Australia map colouring. Variables: WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T. Domains: $\{R, G, B\}$ (Red, Green, Blue). Constraint: adjacent regions must have different colours.

Let us trace AC-3 step by step on the arc (WA, NT):

#### Step 1 — Initialise queue
All directed arcs are queued: (WA,NT), (NT,WA), (WA,SA), (SA,WA), (NT,SA), (SA,NT), (NT,Q), (Q,NT), (SA,Q), (Q,SA), (SA,NSW), (NSW,SA), (Q,NSW), (NSW,Q), (SA,V), (V,SA), (NSW,V), (V,NSW).

#### Step 2 — REVISE(WA, NT)
$D_{WA} = \{R, G, B\}$ and $D_{NT} = \{R, G, B\}$. For each value in $D_{WA}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For } x = R &: \exists\, y \in D_{NT} = \{R, G, B\} \text{ with } y \neq R. \text{ Pick } y = G. \text{ OK.} \\
\text{For } x = G &: \exists\, y \in D_{NT} \text{ with } y \neq G. \text{ Pick } y = R. \text{ OK.} \\
\text{For } x = B &: \exists\, y \in D_{NT} \text{ with } y \neq B. \text{ Pick } y = R. \text{ OK.}
\end{aligned}
$$

No values removed from $D_{WA}$.

#### Step 3 — REVISE(NT, WA)
Similarly, $D_{NT}$ retains all three values.

#### Step 4 — REVISE(WA, SA)
$D_{WA} = \{R, G, B\}$ and $D_{SA} = \{R, G, B\}$. No values removed.

#### Step 5 — Continue through all arcs
After processing all arcs, AC-3 terminates with all domains still equal to $\{R, G, B\}$.

**Inference:** Arc consistency alone is weak here because each variable has only 3 values and Australia is not a tree-structured graph. Backtracking is still required to find a solution. This shows that arc consistency is **necessary but not sufficient**.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Cryptarithmetic — SEND + MORE = MONEY

This is the canonical KTU exam problem. Variables: $\{S, E, N, D, M, O, R, Y\}$. Each is a digit 0–9, with $S \neq 0$, $M \neq 0$, and all distinct.

The column-wise constraints are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D + E &= Y + 10 \cdot c_1 \\
N + R + c_1 &= E + 10 \cdot c_2 \\
E + O + c_2 &= N + 10 \cdot c_3 \\
S + M + c_3 &= O + 10 \cdot c_4 \\
c_4 &= M \quad \text{(carry into ten-thousands column)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step-by-step Backtracking Trace (showing MRV + Forward Checking)

1. **MRV on $M$:** Since $c_4 = M \in \{1\}$ (it must equal the carry out, which can only be 1 because $S+M \le 9+8 = 17$ gives carry of 1), we get $M = 1$.
2. Then $c_4 = 1$, so the carry into the ten-thousands column is satisfied.
3. **MRV on $S$:** $S + 1 + c_3 = O + 10 \cdot c_4$. Since $c_4 = 1$, we need $S + 1 + c_3 = O + 10$. This means $S + c_3 = O + 9$. So $O = S + c_3 - 9$. For $O \in \{0..9\}$, $S = 9$ gives $O = c_3$, and $S = 8$ gives $O = c_3 - 1$, etc. The smallest workable is $S = 9, c_3 = 1 \Rightarrow O = 1$ — but $M = 1$, conflict. Try $S = 9, c_3 = 0 \Rightarrow O = 0$. OK.
4. Continue with column equations to deduce $N + R + c_1 = E + 10 c_2$, etc., eventually arriving at the unique solution:
$$S=9,\; E=5,\; N=6,\; D=7,\; M=1,\; O=0,\; R=8,\; Y=2$$

The proof requires tracing all column equations with their carries $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4 \in \{0, 1\}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Complexity Comparison

| **Algorithm** | **Time Complexity** | **Space** | **Completeness** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backtracking (BT) | $O(d^n)$ worst | $O(n)$ depth | Complete |
| BT + Forward Checking | $O(d^n)$ worst, much faster in practice | $O(n)$ | Complete |
| BT + AC-3 (MAC) | $O(e \cdot d^3)$ per level | $O(e + n)$ | Complete |
| Tree-structured CSP | $O(n \cdot d^2)$ | $O(n)$ | Complete, no BT |
| Min-Conflicts (local search) | $O(d^n)$ worst, fast in practice | $O(n)$ | Incomplete |

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Forward Checking Trace on Australia

Variables: WA, NT, SA, Q, NSW, V, T. Domains: $\{R,G,B\}$.

Assign **WA = R** (chosen by MRV tiebreaker LCV). Forward checking:
- Remove R from $D_{NT}$ → $D_{NT} = \{G, B\}$.
- Remove R from $D_{SA}$ → $D_{SA} = \{G, B\}$.

Assign **NT = G** (LCV heuristic might prefer G if it leaves more options downstream). Forward checking:
- Remove G from $D_{WA}$ → already R, no effect.
- Remove G from $D_{SA}$ → $D_{SA} = \{B\}$.
- Remove G from $D_{Q}$ → $D_{Q} = \{R, B\}$.

Assign **SA = B** (forced). Forward checking:
- Remove B from $D_{WA}, D_{NT}, D_{Q}, D_{NSW}, D_{V}$.

Now $D_Q = \{R\}$ (forced). Cascading:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{Q} &= \{R\} \Rightarrow \text{remove } R \text{ from } D_{NT}, D_{NSW}, D_{SA}.
\end{aligned}
$$

$D_{NSW} = \{G\}$ (forced). Cascading prune of $\{G\}$ from $D_Q, D_V, D_{SA}$. Eventually $D_V = \{R,B\}$ and $D_T = \{R,G,B\}$.

The variable $T$ (Tasmania) has no constraints, so any colour works. Solution found without backtracking.

This demonstrates that **forward checking + MRV + LCV** solves Australia in linear time on this instance, while naïve BT could explore exponentially many paths.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: Australia Map-Colouring Constraint Graph

```mermaid
graph TD
    WA[Western Australia]
    NT[Northern Territory]
    SA[South Australia]
    Q[Queensland]
    NSW[New South Wales]
    V[Victoria]
    T[Tasmania]

    WA --- NT
    WA --- SA
    NT --- SA
    NT --- Q
    SA --- Q
    SA --- NSW
    SA --- V
    Q --- NSW
    NSW --- V
```

> *Figure 4.1 — Binary constraint graph for Australia map colouring. Tasmania has no edges because it is not adjacent to the mainland.*

### 4.2 Diagram 2: AC-3 Algorithm Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start AC-3 with all arcs in queue] --> B{Queue empty?}
    B -- No --> C[Pop arc Xi Xj from queue]
    C --> D[Call REVISE on Xi Xj]
    D --> E{Domain of Xi changed?}
    E -- Yes --> F{Domain of Xi empty?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Return FAILURE]
    F -- No --> H[Add arc Xk Xi for every neighbour Xk of Xi excluding Xj to queue]
    H --> B
    E -- No --> B
    B -- Yes --> I[Return CSP with reduced domains]
```

> *Figure 4.2 — Control flow of the AC-3 algorithm. The "domain changed?" check is the central feedback loop.*

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Backtracking Search with Inference (MAC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: All variables unassigned] --> B[Pick unassigned variable via MRV]
    B --> C[Order domain values via LCV]
    C --> D{Any values left?}
    D -- No --> Z[BACKTRACK: return failure]
    D -- Yes --> E[Assign variable to next value]
    E --> F[Run inference: AC-3 or Forward Checking]
    F --> G{Inference failed?}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> H{All variables assigned?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Return complete assignment SOLUTION]
    H -- No --> B
    Z --> C
```

> *Figure 4.3 — Backtracking search augmented with MRV, LCV, and inference. The "BACKTRACK" arrow loops back to try the next value for the previous variable.*

### 4.4 Diagram 4: CSP Hierarchy of Inference Strength

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node Consistency] --> B[Arc Consistency AC-3]
    B --> C[Path Consistency]
    B --> D[Forward Checking]
    B --> E[Full Lookahead MAC]
    C --> F[k-Consistency]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Global Consistency solves CSP without search]
```

> *Figure 4.4 — Hierarchy showing how stronger consistency levels require exponentially more computation but eliminate more search.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a Constraint Satisfaction Problem (CSP). List its components.**

**Model Answer:**
A CSP is defined by a triple $(X, D, C)$ where:
- $X = \{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n\}$ is a set of **variables**.
- $D = \{D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n\}$ is a set of **domains**, each $D_i$ being the set of possible values for $X_i$.
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m\}$ is a set of **constraints**, each restricting the values that a tuple of variables may take simultaneously.

A state of the problem is defined by an assignment of values to some or all variables, and a **solution** is a complete assignment that satisfies all constraints.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024] What is arc consistency? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
A directed arc $(X_i, X_j)$ is **arc-consistent** if for every value $x \in D_i$, there exists some value $y \in D_j$ such that the binary constraint between $X_i$ and $X_j$ is satisfied by $(x, y)$.

**Example:** If $X_1 \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $X_2 \in \{2, 3\}$ with constraint $X_1 < X_2$, then for $x=3$, we need $y > 3$ but no such $y$ exists. So $3$ is removed from $D_1$ via REVISE$(X_1, X_2)$, leaving $D_1 = \{1, 2\}$. The arc is now consistent.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not skip REVISE steps.** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by saying "AC-3 is run" without showing *which arcs are processed* and *which values are deleted*. Always trace the queue explicitly.
> 2. **Do not confuse node, arc, and path consistency.** These are nested — arc consistency does not imply path consistency.
> 3. **In backtracking questions, always state the heuristics being used** (MRV, LCV, Forward Checking). Examiners explicitly check whether you named them.
> 4. **Cryptarithmetic carries ($c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$) must be declared** as binary variables or noted as 0/1 integers. Marks are deducted if carries are silently ignored.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain Constraint Propagation in Constraint Satisfaction Problems. Describe the AC-3 algorithm in detail with its time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the AC-3 algorithm to the following CSP and show the resulting domains: Variables $A, B, C$ with domains $D_A = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $D_B = \{1, 2, 3\}$, $D_C = \{1, 2, 3\}$. Constraints: $A < B$ and $B < C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Constraint Propagation & AC-3:**
- Constraint propagation is the use of constraints to *reduce* the domains of variables without performing search. Arc consistency is the most common form.
- **AC-3 pseudocode** (must be written in answer):

```
function AC-3(csp):
    queue ← {(X_i, X_j) | (X_i, X_j) is an arc in csp}
    while queue ≠ ∅:
        (X_i, X_j) ← POP(queue)
        if REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
            if size(D_i) == 0: return FAILURE
            for each X_k in NEIGHBOURS[X_i] \ {X_j}:
                queue.ADD((X_k, X_i))
    return csp

function REVISE(csp, X_i, X_j):
    revised ← false
    for each x in D_i:
        if no y in D_j satisfies constraint(X_i, X_j):
            DELETE x from D_i
            revised ← true
    return revised
```

- **Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial queue size} &= O(e) \quad (e = \text{number of arcs}) \\
\text{Each arc added at most } d \text{ times} &\Rightarrow \text{total pop operations} = O(ed) \\
\text{Cost of each REVISE} &= O(d^2) \\
\therefore \text{Total time} &= O(e \cdot d^3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) AC-3 Trace on $A < B$ and $B < C$ with $D_A = D_B = D_C = \{1,2,3\}$:**

Initial queue: $\{(A,B), (B,A), (B,C), (C,B)\}$.

**Step 1:** Pop $(A,B)$. REVISE$(A,B)$: For $x \in \{1,2,3\}$, is there $y > x$?
- $x=1$: $y \in \{2,3\}$ works. Keep.
- $x=2$: $y \in \{3\}$ works. Keep.
- $x=3$: No $y > 3$. **Delete 3 from $D_A$.**
$D_A = \{1, 2\}$. Re-add $(C,A)$ — no, $A$ and $C$ not constrained.

**Step 2:** Pop $(B,A)$. REVISE$(B,A)$: For $y \in D_B$, is there $x \in D_A = \{1,2\}$ with $x < y$?
- $y=1$: need $x < 1$. No such $x$. **Delete 1 from $D_B$.**
$D_B = \{2, 3\}$.

**Step 3:** Pop $(B,C)$. REVISE$(B,C)$: For $y \in D_B = \{2,3\}$, is there $z \in D_C = \{1,2,3\}$ with $y < z$?
- $y=2$: $z=3$ works. Keep.
- $y=3$: No $z > 3$. **Delete 3 from $D_B$.**
$D_B = \{2\}$.

**Step 4:** Pop $(C,B)$. REVISE$(C,B)$: For $z \in D_C = \{1,2,3\}$, is there $y \in D_B = \{2\}$ with $y < z$?
- $z=1$: $y=2 > 1$. **Delete 1 from $D_C$.**
- $z=2$: $y=2 < 2$ false. **Delete 2 from $D_C$.**
- $z=3$: $y=2 < 3$. Keep.
$D_C = \{3\}$.

**Step 5:** Queue empty. Final domains: $D_A = \{1, 2\}$, $D_B = \{2\}$, $D_C = \{3\}$. **Solution exists and is unique: $A=2, B=2$ — wait, but $A < B$ requires $A \ne B$. Recheck:**

$D_B = \{2\}$ means $B=2$ is the only value, but $A \in \{1,2\}$ and $A < 2 \Rightarrow A = 1$. Solution: $A=1, B=2, C=3$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** What are the different types of inferences used in CSPs? Explain Forward Checking and the AC-3 algorithm as inference mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the SEND + MORE = MONEY cryptarithmetic puzzle using backtracking search with MRV and Forward Checking heuristics. Show all carries and variable assignments step by step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Inferences in CSPs:**

The standard inferences used by CSP solvers to prune the search space are:

1. **Node Consistency:** For unary constraints, simply remove violating values from the variable's domain.
2. **Arc Consistency (AC-3):** As described above, ensures for every $x \in D_i$ there is a supporting $y \in D_j$.
3. **Path Consistency:** Strengthens AC by ensuring that for every pair $(x, z)$ consistent with a value $y$ in the middle, there is a value $y$ that allows both arcs to be satisfied simultaneously.
4. **Forward Checking:** When a variable $X$ is assigned, look at every unassigned variable $Y$ connected to $X$ by a constraint, and delete from $D_Y$ any value that is inconsistent with the value assigned to $X$.
5. **MAC (Maintaining Arc Consistency):** After each assignment, run AC-3 on the remaining variables — strictly stronger than Forward Checking.

**(b) SEND + MORE = MONEY — Backtracking Trace:**

Variables and domains: $\{S, E, N, D, M, O, R, Y\}$, each in $\{0..9\}$, all distinct, $S \ne 0$, $M \ne 0$.

Column equations with carries $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4 \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D + E &= Y + 10 c_1 \tag{col 1} \\
N + R + c_1 &= E + 10 c_2 \tag{col 2} \\
E + O + c_2 &= N + 10 c_3 \tag{col 3} \\
S + M + c_3 &= O + 10 c_4 \tag{col 4} \\
c_4 &= M \tag{col 5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MRV Step 1:** Variable $M$ appears most constrained; column 5 forces $c_4 = M \in \{1\}$ (cannot be 0 since $S + M + c_3 \le 17$ gives carry $\le 1$, and $M \ne 0$).
So $M = 1$, $c_4 = 1$.

**Forward checking:** Remove 1 from all other variable domains.

**MRV Step 2:** Variable $S$ now has the highest constraint coupling. From column 4: $S + 1 + c_3 = O + 10$. So $O = S + c_3 - 9$. With $S \in \{0..9\} \setminus \{1\}$ and $O \in \{0..9\} \setminus \{1\}$:
- $S = 9, c_3 = 1 \Rightarrow O = 1$ — conflict with $M$.
- $S = 9, c_3 = 0 \Rightarrow O = 0$. **Try this.**

So $S = 9$, $c_3 = 0$, $O = 0$.

**Forward checking:** Remove 0 and 9 from all unassigned variables.

**MRV Step 3:** Column 3 gives $E + 0 + c_2 = N + 0$, i.e., $E + c_2 = N$. So $N = E + c_2$.

Column 2 gives $N + R + c_1 = E + 10 c_2$. Substituting $N = E + c_2$:
$(E + c_2) + R + c_1 = E + 10 c_2$, i.e., $R = 9 c_2 - c_1$.

Since $R \in \{0..9\}$ and $c_1, c_2 \in \{0, 1\}$:
- $c_2 = 1, c_1 = 0 \Rightarrow R = 9$. **Conflict** ($S = 9$ already).
- $c_2 = 1, c_1 = 1 \Rightarrow R = 8$. **Try this.**

So $c_2 = 1$, $c_1 = 1$, $R = 8$. Then $N = E + 1$.

**Forward checking:** Remove 8 from unassigned.

Column 1 gives $D + E = Y + 10$, so $Y = D + E - 10$. Since $Y \in \{0..9\}$, we need $D + E \ge 10$.

Also $N = E + 1$ with all-different and $N \in \{2..7\}$ (since $S=9, M=1, O=0, R=8$ used). So $E \in \{2..6\}$.

**Try $E = 5$** (MRV): $N = 6$. Remaining digits for $D, Y$: $\{2, 3, 4, 7\}$. Column 1: $D + 5 = Y + 10$, so $Y = D - 5$. With $D \in \{2,3,4,7\}$:
- $D = 7 \Rightarrow Y = 2$. **Try this.**

All distinct: $S=9, E=5, N=6, D=7, M=1, O=0, R=8, Y=2$. ✓

**Verify:** SEND = 9567, MORE = 1068, MONEY = 10635. $9567 + 1068 = 10635$. ✓

**Final Solution:** $S=9, E=5, N=6, D=7, M=1, O=0, R=8, Y=2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU 2024:**

- **CSP Definition:** Triple $(X, D, C)$. Solution = complete assignment satisfying all constraints.
- **Constraint Graph:** Nodes are variables, edges are binary constraints.
- **Arc Consistency (AC):** $\forall x \in D_i,\; \exists y \in D_j$ such that constraint holds.
- **AC-3 complexity:** $O(e \cdot d^3)$ where $e$ = number of arcs and $d$ = max domain size.
- **MRV heuristic:** Fail-first — choose variable with fewest remaining values.
- **LCV heuristic:** Choose value that prunes fewest options for neighbours.
- **Degree heuristic:** Tie-break MRV using variable with most constraints on remaining vars.
- **Forward Checking:** When a variable is assigned, prune its neighbours' domains.
- **MAC (Maintaining Arc Consistency):** Run AC-3 after every assignment — strongest inference.
- **Backtracking is complete** but exponential; heuristics make it tractable in practice.
- **Tree-structured CSPs** are solvable in $O(n \cdot d^2)$ — no backtracking required.
- **Cryptarithmetic carries** must be declared as binary/0-1 integers; 5-column problem has 4 carries $c_1, c_2, c_3, c_4$.
- **All-different constraint:** Each variable must take a unique value (essential in cryptarithmetic).
- **Common pitfall:** Forgetting that AC does NOT solve the CSP — it only reduces the search space.
- **Real-world uses:** Map colouring, scheduling, configuration, register allocation, Sudoku.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Knowledge, Logic, and Reasoning Patterns: Knowledge Based Agents, The Wumpus World
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Knowledge, Logic, and Reasoning Patterns: Knowledge Based Agents, The Wumpus World

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Knowledge-Based Agents (KBAs) — Formal Definition

A **Knowledge-Based Agent (KBA)** is a rational agent whose decision-making is driven by a structured, explicit, and manipulable **Knowledge Base (KB)** of sentences expressed in a formal representation language, combined with an **Inference Engine** that derives new conclusions through logical deduction.

Formally, a KBA can be defined as the tuple:

$$
\text{KBA} = \langle KB, TELL, ASK, \text{Action}, \text{Percept} \rangle
$$

Where:
- **KB** = Set of sentences representing what the agent *knows* (its knowledge state).
- **TELL(sentence)** = Operation that *adds* a sentence $s$ to the KB.
- **ASK(query)** = Operation that *queries* the KB to derive answers based on its contents.
- The agent's behaviour follows the abstract function: $f: \text{Percept}^* \rightarrow \text{Action}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** A knowledge-based agent maintains a KB and an inference system. The agent's action is chosen by **logical entailment**, not by hard-coded condition-action rules. This makes the agent *flexible*, *explainable*, and *adaptive* to new scenarios.

### 1.2 The Wumpus World — Formal Definition

The **Wumpus World** is a classic AI toy problem used to study logical reasoning in partially observable, stochastic environments. It is a **4×4 cave grid** populated with hazards and rewards, where the agent must navigate using *only logical inference* from its percepts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Wumpus World critical for KTU exams?**
> It is a direct application of propositional logic, KB construction, and inference — all **core KTU 2024 syllabus items**. Expect either a 3-mark direct question or a 7–14 mark scenario-based question.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Blindfolded Detective:**
Imagine a detective is blindfolded in a mansion. He can feel the air (breeze), smell (stench), and hear sounds, but cannot see. Based purely on these partial sensory inputs, he must deduce which rooms are safe and which are dangerous. He keeps a *notebook* (the Knowledge Base) where he writes down what he has learned. After every observation, he pauses to *reason* about the notebook's contents and decides his next move. This is exactly how a knowledge-based agent in the Wumpus World operates.

**Analogy 2 — The GPS Navigator:**
Your smartphone's GPS does not hard-code every road. Instead, it stores facts (a map) and rules (shortest-path algorithms). When you ask "How do I reach the airport?", it *infers* the path by applying rules to facts. A KBA follows the same principle — facts are the percepts, rules are the world's logic, and the inference engine is the GPS algorithm.

### 1.4 Three Levels of a Knowledge-Based Agent

| Level | Component | Human Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Knowledge Level** | What the agent *knows* and what its *goals* are | A scientist's understanding |
| **Logical Level** | How the knowledge is *encoded* (e.g., into logic sentences) | Writing equations on a board |
| **Implementation Level** | The *data structures and algorithms* storing the KB | Computer hardware and code |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between these three levels. Memorize: **Knowledge = "what", Logic = "how to encode", Implementation = "where it lives in memory"**.

### 1.5 Generic KBA Architecture — Function Skeleton

The agent maintains a KB and updates it as follows:

$$
\text{KB} \leftarrow \text{KB} \cup \text{Percept-As-Sentence}
$$
$$
\text{Action} \leftarrow \text{ASK}(\text{KB}, \text{``what action should I take?''})
$$
$$
\text{KB} \leftarrow \text{KB} \cup \text{Action-As-Sentence}
$$
$$
\text{Execute(Action)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU-mandated generic KBA loop**. Even if a specific agent has additional components (planning, learning), the *core loop* is always: **Observe → TELL → ASK → Act → TELL again**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Knowledge-Based Agent

A KBA is composed of two core subsystems:

1. **Knowledge Base (KB)** — A *set of sentences* in a formal language.
2. **Inference Engine** — *Procedural* domain-independent algorithms that derive new sentences.

#### 2.1.1 Axioms vs Derived Sentences
- **Axioms**: Sentences provided initially (background knowledge + percepts).
- **Derived Sentences**: Sentences obtained by applying inference rules to existing ones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world systems, the KB is analogous to an *Ontology* in the Semantic Web, a *Rule Base* in expert systems (e.g., MYCIN, DENDRAL), or a *Facts Table* in modern Knowledge Graphs used by Google, Alexa, and IBM Watson.

### 2.2 Two Approaches to Building a KBA

| Approach | Description | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Declarative** | Tell the agent *what* it needs to know; let the inference engine figure out the *how*. | Easy to add new knowledge incrementally. | Inference can be slow or undecidable. |
| **Procedural** | Encode desired behaviours directly as code/rules. | Fast execution. | Hard to debug, modify, or scale. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus strongly favours the **declarative approach** because it is the foundation of modern symbolic AI, Knowledge Graphs, and Explainable AI (XAI).

### 2.3 Properties of a Good Representation Language

For KTU, memorize these five desirable properties:

1. **Representational Adequacy** — Can express everything needed in the domain.
2. **Inferential Adequacy** — Allows new knowledge to be derived.
3. **Inferential Efficiency** — Inference is computationally tractable.
4. **Learnability** — New knowledge can be acquired by the agent itself.
5. **Naturalness / Clarity** — Easy for humans to read and write.

### 2.4 The Wumpus World — PEAS Specification

PEAS = **P**erformance, **E**nvironment, **A**ctuators, **S**ensors.

#### P — Performance Measure
- **+1000** for climbing out with the gold.
- **−1000** for falling into a pit or being eaten by the Wumpus.
- **−1** for each action taken.
- **−10** for using the arrow (only one arrow available).

#### E — Environment
- A **4×4 grid** of rooms (16 cells total).
- Adjacent rooms (up/down/left/right only — **no diagonals**).
- Wumpus, gold, and pits are placed randomly, subject to constraints:
  - The square **[1,1]** is always safe.
  - The Wumpus is in exactly one square (not [1,1]).
  - Each square other than [1,1] may contain a pit with probability **0.2**.

#### A — Actuators
The agent can perform **5 actions**:
1. `MoveForward`
2. `TurnLeft` (90°)
3. `TurnRight` (90°)
4. `Grab` (picks up gold if in the same square)
5. `Shoot` (arrow travels straight until it hits the Wumpus or a wall)
6. `Climb` (only meaningful at the start square [1,1])

#### S — Sensors (Percepts)
The agent receives a 5-element percept vector: $\langle \text{Stench}, \text{Breeze}, \text{Glitter}, \text{Bump}, \text{Scream} \rangle$

- **Stench** = Wumpus is in an *adjacent* square.
- **Breeze** = A pit is in an *adjacent* square.
- **Glitter** = Gold is in the *current* square.
- **Bump** = The agent just hit a wall.
- **Scream** = The Wumpus was hit by the arrow (it dies).

### 2.5 Environment Properties (KTU Favourite Question)

| Property | Classification | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Fully vs Partially Observable | **Partially Observable** | Agent can only sense adjacent squares. |
| Deterministic vs Stochastic | **Deterministic** (rules are fixed) but outcomes are partially unknown | Percepts are deterministic given the world state. |
| Episodic vs Sequential | **Sequential** | Decisions affect all future states. |
| Static vs Dynamic | **Static** | Environment does not change while the agent deliberates. |
| Discrete vs Continuous | **Discrete** | Grid, discrete percepts, discrete actions. |
| Single vs Multi-Agent | **Single Agent** | The Wumpus is an environmental hazard, not a rational agent. |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Logical Vocabulary (Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **must-memorize cheat sheet** for Module 4 questions.

| Symbol | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| $\neg P$ | NOT P (negation) | $\neg W_{1,3}$ = "No Wumpus at [1,3]" |
| $P \wedge Q$ | P AND Q (conjunction) | $B_{1,2} \wedge S_{1,2}$ |
| $P \vee Q$ | P OR Q (disjunction) | $P_{1,2} \vee P_{2,1}$ |
| $P \Rightarrow Q$ | P implies Q | $W_{1,3} \Rightarrow \neg S_{2,3}$ |
| $P \Leftrightarrow Q$ | P iff Q (biconditional) | $B_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow (P_{1,1} \vee P_{2,2} \vee P_{1,3})$ |
| $KB \models \alpha$ | KB *entails* $\alpha$ | "KB logically implies $\alpha$" |
| $KB \vdash_i \alpha$ | $\alpha$ is *derived* from KB by algorithm $i$ | "$\alpha$ is provable using inference procedure $i$" |

### 2.7 The Fundamental Equation of Logical Entailment

$$
KB \models \alpha \quad \text{iff} \quad M(KB) \subseteq M(\alpha)
$$

Where $M(X)$ denotes the set of all models (interpretations/worlds) in which $X$ is true.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English:** "$\alpha$ follows from the KB if every model that satisfies the KB also satisfies $\alpha$." This is the *semantic* definition of entailment — the *syntactic* counterpart is *derivability* ($\vdash$), used by the inference engine.

### 2.8 Two Key Inference Algorithms in Wumpus World

- **Modus Ponens**: From $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha$, infer $\beta$.
- **And-Elimination**: From $\alpha_1 \wedge \alpha_2 \wedge \ldots \wedge \alpha_n$, infer any $\alpha_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, you must also know **Resolution** (a complete inference procedure) and **Forward / Backward Chaining** (used in rule-based systems). Resolution is the standard inference for the Wumpus World KB.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Example

### 3.1 Sample Wumpus World Configuration (Standard KTU Reference)

For consistency, we use the canonical example below throughout this section. Coordinates $(x, y)$ have $x$ = column (left→right), $y$ = row (bottom→top). The agent starts at **[1,1]** facing East.

| Object | Position |
|---|---|
| Agent start | $[1,1]$ |
| Wumpus | $[1,3]$ |
| Pit A | $[3,1]$ |
| Pit B | $[3,3]$ |
| Gold | $[2,3]$ |

### 3.2 Percept Sequence (Deterministic Reasoning Walkthrough)

The agent visits cells one by one, accumulating percepts. Below is the step-by-step knowledge buildup.

#### Step 0: Initial KB

The KB starts with the rules of the environment (causal rules) plus the start percept (None — [1,1] is always safe).

$$
KB_0 = \big\{ \neg P_{1,1}, \neg W_{1,1} \big\}
$$

> The agent observes **No Stench, No Breeze** at [1,1]. This is *consistent* with [1,1] being safe.

#### Step 1: Move to [2,1] → Percept = (Breeze, No Stench)

The breeze tells the agent that *some adjacent square* has a pit. The adjacent squares of [2,1] are [1,1], [3,1], [2,2].

$$
KB_1 = KB_0 \cup \big\{ B_{2,1} \big\}
$$

The breeze rule (causal): $B_{2,1} \Leftrightarrow (P_{1,1} \vee P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1})$.

By **And-Elimination** and the known fact $\neg P_{1,1}$, we simplify:

$$
P_{1,1} \vee P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1} \quad \text{and} \quad \neg P_{1,1}
$$

Therefore, by **Unit Resolution**:

$$
KB_1 \models (P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1})
$$

> The agent *cannot yet conclude which one*, but it *knows at least one is a pit*. Both [1,2] and [2,2] remain *possibly safe* (because [1,1] is known safe, and the agent has not been told about [2,2] being a pit).

#### Step 2: Move to [1,2] → Percept = (Stench, No Breeze)

Now the agent perceives a *stench* (Wumpus adjacent) and *no breeze* (no pit adjacent).

Adjacent squares of [1,2] are [1,1], [1,3], [2,2].

$$
KB_2 = KB_1 \cup \big\{ S_{1,2}, \neg B_{1,2} \big\}
$$

Stench rule: $S_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow (W_{1,1} \vee W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2})$.
Since $\neg W_{1,1}$ is known, we infer:

$$
W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}
$$

No-breeze rule: $\neg B_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow \neg (P_{1,1} \vee P_{1,3} \vee P_{2,2})$.
Since $\neg P_{1,1}$ is known, this simplifies to:

$$
\neg P_{1,3} \wedge \neg P_{2,2}
$$

So the agent now knows that **[1,3] and [2,2] have no pits**.

#### Step 3: Combine KB from Steps 1 and 2

Recall from $KB_1$: $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$.
From $KB_2$: $\neg P_{2,2}$.

By **Unit Resolution** with $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$ and $\neg P_{2,2}$:

$$
KB_2 \models P_{3,1}
$$

So the agent now **knows with certainty that [3,1] contains a pit**. The cell [2,2] is **proven safe** (no Wumpus and no pit).

#### Step 4: Move to [2,2] → Percept = (No Stench, No Breeze)

$$
KB_3 = KB_2 \cup \big\{ \neg S_{2,2}, \neg B_{2,2} \big\}
$$

From $\neg S_{2,2}$: $\neg W_{1,2} \wedge \neg W_{2,1} \wedge \neg W_{2,3} \wedge \neg W_{3,2}$.
Combined with previous: $W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}$ and $\neg W_{2,2}$, hence $W_{1,3}$!

The agent now **knows the Wumpus is at [1,3]**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Knowledge State at Each Step

| Visited Cell | Percept | New Sentences Added | New Inferred Facts | Safe Cells Identified | Pit/Wumpus Located |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [1,1] | None | $\neg P_{1,1}, \neg W_{1,1}$ | — | [1,1] | None |
| [2,1] | Breeze | $B_{2,1}$ | $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$ | — | — |
| [1,2] | Stench, $\neg$Breeze | $S_{1,2}, \neg B_{1,2}$ | $W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}$, $\neg P_{1,3}, \neg P_{2,2}$ | — | — |
| [1,2] + [2,1] combined | — | — | $P_{3,1}$ | [2,2] | Pit at [3,1] |
| [2,2] | None | $\neg S_{2,2}, \neg B_{2,2}$ | $W_{1,3}$ | [2,2] confirmed safe | Wumpus at [1,3] |

### 3.4 Mathematical Derivation — Why Resolution Works

**Resolution Rule (single-step):**

$$
\frac{\ell_1 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_k, \quad m_1 \vee \cdots \vee m_n}{\ell_1 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_{i-1} \vee \ell_{i+1} \vee \cdots \vee \ell_k \vee m_1 \vee \cdots \vee m_{j-1} \vee m_{j+1} \vee \cdots \vee m_n}
$$

where $\ell_i$ and $m_j$ are *complementary literals* (i.e., $\ell_i = \neg m_j$).

**Example from the Wumpus KB:**

Given the two clauses in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):

$$
C_1 = P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}
$$
$$
C_2 = \neg P_{2,2}
$$

We identify complementary literals: $P_{2,2}$ in $C_1$ and $\neg P_{2,2}$ in $C_2$. Applying resolution:

$$
\text{Res}(C_1, C_2) = P_{3,1}
$$

Thus, by one resolution step, we *derive* $P_{3,1}$ from the KB.

### 3.5 Generalization — From $KB$ to Action via Entailment

The action selection in a KBA can be formalized as:

$$
\text{Action}^* = \underset{a \in \text{Actions}}{\arg\max} \; KB \models \text{Preconditions}(a)
$$

That is, the agent chooses the action whose preconditions are *entailed* by the current KB. If the KB entails "$\neg P_{2,2} \wedge \neg W_{2,2}$", then `MoveForward` to [2,2] is *safe* and selected.

### 3.6 Worked Example — Complete KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem:** In a 4×4 Wumpus World, the agent starts at [1,1] facing East. After moving to [2,1], it perceives a Breeze. After moving back to [1,1] and then to [1,2], it perceives Stench and no Breeze.
(a) Construct the KB after each move.
(b) Determine which cells the agent can mark as *safe* and which as *unsafe*.

**Solution:**

**(a) KB Construction:**

$KB_0 = \{ \neg P_{1,1}, \neg W_{1,1} \}$

After moving to [2,1] with Breeze: $KB_1 = KB_0 \cup \{B_{2,1}\}$.
Breeze rule: $B_{2,1} \Leftrightarrow (P_{1,1} \vee P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1})$.
With $\neg P_{1,1}$, infer: $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$.

After moving to [1,2] with Stench and no Breeze: $KB_2 = KB_1 \cup \{S_{1,2}, \neg B_{1,2}\}$.
Stench rule: $S_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow (W_{1,1} \vee W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2})$.
With $\neg W_{1,1}$, infer: $W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}$.
No-breeze rule: $\neg(P_{1,1} \vee P_{1,3} \vee P_{2,2})$.
With $\neg P_{1,1}$, infer: $\neg P_{1,3} \wedge \neg P_{2,2}$.

Combining $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$ with $\neg P_{2,2}$ via resolution: $KB_2 \models P_{3,1}$.

**(b) Safety Analysis:**

- **Safe (proved):** [1,1], [1,2] (visited), and [2,2] (no pit, no Wumpus by inference).
- **Unsafe (proved):** [3,1] is a pit. [1,3] could be a Wumpus or pit (combined with $W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}$, since $\neg W_{2,2}$ is true, then $W_{1,3}$ holds, but [1,3] is unsafe because of Wumpus).
- **Unknown:** [3,2], [2,3], [1,4], [2,4], [3,3], [3,4], [4,1], [4,2], [4,3], [4,4] — no information yet.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of a Generic Knowledge-Based Agent (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Percept from Environment] --> B[Percept Processing]
    B --> C[TELL: Add Sentence to KB]
    C --> D[Knowledge Base KB]
    D --> E[Inference Engine]
    E --> F{ASK: Query KB}
    F --> G[Action Selection]
    G --> H[Actuator Executes Action]
    H --> I[Environment]
    I --> A
    D --> J[Domain Rules Axioms]
    J --> D
    E --> K[Modus Ponens / Resolution / Chaining]
    K --> E
    style D fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two yellow/blue boxes are the *intelligence* of the agent. The red boxes are the *environment interface*. Everything else is glue.

### 4.2 The Wumpus World Grid Layout (Sample Configuration)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph World["Wumpus World 4x4 Grid - Agent View After Step 3"]
        direction TB
        Row4["y=4: [1,4]OK   [2,4]OK   [3,4]?   [4,4]?"]
        Row3["y=3: [1,3]W!   [2,3]G    [3,3]P    [4,3]?"]
        Row2["y=2: [1,2]A,S  [2,2]OK   [3,2]?    [4,2]?"]
        Row1["y=1: [1,1]OK   [2,1]A,B  [3,1]P    [4,1]?"]
    end
    style Row3 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b
    style Row2 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#239b56
    style Row1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#239b56
    style Row4 fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
```

**Legend:**
- `A` = Agent (current or past location)
- `S` = Stench perceived
- `B` = Breeze perceived
- `W!` = Wumpus (dangerous — do not enter)
- `P` = Pit (dangerous — do not enter)
- `G` = Gold (goal — must grab and return)
- `OK` = Safe (proved)
- `?` = Unknown

### 4.3 Knowledge Inference Pipeline in the Wumpus World

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Agent at 1,1]) --> Step1[Move to 2,1]
    Step1 --> P1[Percept: Breeze]
    P1 --> R1[Add B_2,1 to KB]
    R1 --> S1[Infer: P_2,2 OR P_3,1]
    S1 --> Step2[Move to 1,2]
    Step2 --> P2[Percept: Stench, NOT Breeze]
    P2 --> R2[Add S_1,2 and NOT B_1,2]
    R2 --> S2[Infer: NOT P_1,3 AND NOT P_2,2]
    S2 --> S3[Infer: P_3,1 by Resolution]
    S3 --> S4[Infer: W_1,3 by Resolution]
    S4 --> Step3[Move to 2,2 - Confirmed Safe]
    Step3 --> Done([KB Complete: Wumpus, Pits, Safe cells all identified])
    style S1 fill:#fff3cd
    style S2 fill:#fff3cd
    style S3 fill:#d4edda
    style S4 fill:#d4edda
    style Done fill:#cce5ff
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of KB Inference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Percept Layer: Sensors Input] --> L2[Symbolic Encoder: Translate Percepts to Logic Sentences]
    L2 --> L3[Knowledge Base Storage: Set of CNF Clauses]
    L3 --> L4[Inference Engine: Resolution and Modus Ponens]
    L4 --> L5[Belief Revision Module: Update KB with Derived Facts]
    L5 --> L6[Action Selector: Choose Best Safe Action]
    L6 --> L7[Actuator Layer: Execute in Environment]
    L7 --> L1
    L3 -.-> L4
    L5 -.-> L3
    style L3 fill:#3498db,color:#fff,stroke:#1a5276,stroke-width:3px
    style L4 fill:#e74c3c,color:#fff,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard KTU exam diagram** to draw when asked "Explain the architecture of a knowledge-based agent with a block diagram." Memorize the **5 layers**: Sensors → Encoder → KB → Inference → Action.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
