## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define a knowledge-based agent. List its main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A knowledge-based agent is a rational agent that uses a **Knowledge Base (KB)** to store information about the world in a formal representation language and an **inference engine** to derive new conclusions and decide actions.

**Main components:**
1. **Knowledge Base (KB)** — Stores sentences (facts and rules) about the world.
2. **Inference Engine** — Derives new sentences from existing ones using logical rules.
3. **TELL–ASK Interface** — Allows the agent to add new sentences and query the KB.
4. **Learning/Updating Module** — Updates the KB based on new percepts.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components — full credit requires at least 3 components.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What are the percepts available to an agent in the Wumpus World? Explain each briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The agent receives a 5-element percept vector at each time step:

1. **Stench** — Perceived in squares adjacent to the Wumpus.
2. **Breeze** — Perceived in squares adjacent to a pit.
3. **Glitter** — Perceived in the square containing the gold.
4. **Bump** — Perceived when the agent hits a wall (trying to move outside the grid).
5. **Scream** — Perceived when the Wumpus is killed by the arrow.

*(1 mark for listing, 2 marks for any 4 correct explanations.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **generic knowledge-based agent**. Discuss the role of the TELL and ASK operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Wumpus World environment** in detail. Specify the PEAS (Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors) description and the rules of the game. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — KBA Architecture (7 marks)

A knowledge-based agent is built around two core structures: the **Knowledge Base (KB)** and the **Inference Engine**.

**Architecture (3 marks):**

Refer to the block diagram from **Section 4.4** (Block-Level Functional Architecture).

**The generic loop is:**

1. The agent **perceives** the environment through sensors.
2. The **TELL** operation adds the percept (translated into a logic sentence) to the KB.
3. The **ASK** operation queries the KB about which action to take.
4. The chosen action is **executed** via actuators.
5. The action is also **TELL**ed back to the KB (so the agent remembers what it did).

**Role of TELL (2 marks):**
- Adds new sentences to the KB.
- Used for: initial knowledge, percepts, and the agent's own actions.
- Effectively "teaches" the agent about the world.

**Role of ASK (2 marks):**
- Queries the KB to derive answers to questions like "Which action should I take?".
- Internally, it uses the inference engine to compute the answer.
- Returns a derived sentence, not a stored one.

#### Part (b) — Wumpus World PEAS (7 marks)

**Performance Measure (2 marks):**
- **+1000** for climbing out of the cave with the gold.
- **−1000** for falling into a pit or being eaten by the Wumpus.
- **−1** for each action.
- **−10** for shooting the arrow.

**Environment (2 marks):**
- A 4×4 grid of rooms with walls.
- One Wumpus (stationary, kills agent on contact).
- Some pits (immovable, kill agent on contact).
- One piece of gold.
- The agent starts at square **[1,1]** facing East.

**Actuators (1.5 marks):**
`MoveForward`, `TurnLeft`, `TurnRight`, `Grab`, `Shoot`, `Climb`.

**Sensors (1.5 marks):**
The 5-element percept vector — Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the Wumpus World is **partially observable** (the agent cannot see beyond its current square). Also, students confuse the *arrow* — there is only **one arrow**, and the Wumpus can be killed only by a direct hit from it. If the Wumpus dies, all its stench disappears permanently.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Define **entailment** and **inference** in the context of knowledge representation. Distinguish between $KB \models \alpha$ and $KB \vdash \alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 4×4 Wumpus World where the agent starts at [1,1]. After visiting [2,1], the agent perceives a Breeze. After returning to [1,1] and moving to [1,2], the agent perceives a Stench but no Breeze. Construct the Knowledge Base step-by-step and identify the cells that can be marked as **safe** with logical justification. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Entailment vs Inference (7 marks)

**Definition of Entailment (2 marks):**
A sentence $\alpha$ is *entailed* by a knowledge base $KB$ (written $KB \models \alpha$) if and only if $\alpha$ is true in **every model** (possible world) in which $KB$ is true.

**Definition of Inference (2 marks):**
A sentence $\alpha$ is *derived* from $KB$ by an inference procedure $i$ (written $KB \vdash_i \alpha$) if the procedure can produce $\alpha$ from $KB$ using a defined set of rules.

**Distinction (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Entailment ($\models$) | Inference ($\vdash$) |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | **Semantic** (about truth in models) | **Syntactic** (about symbol manipulation) |
| Definition | $M(KB) \subseteq M(\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ is produced by rules applied to $KB$ |
| Computability | Generally not computable (requires model checking) | Computable (algorithm runs in finite time) |
| Soundness | Always correct *by definition* | An inference procedure is *sound* if $KB \vdash_i \alpha \Rightarrow KB \models \alpha$ |
| Completeness | A procedure is *complete* if $KB \models \alpha \Rightarrow KB \vdash_i \alpha$ | May or may not be complete |

#### Part (b) — KB Construction and Safety Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1: Initial KB at [1,1] (1 mark)**
$KB_0 = \{ \neg P_{1,1}, \neg W_{1,1} \}$

**Step 2: After moving to [2,1] with Breeze (2 marks)**
$KB_1 = KB_0 \cup \{B_{2,1}\}$
Breeze rule: $B_{2,1} \Leftrightarrow (P_{1,1} \vee P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1})$
With $\neg P_{1,1}$ → $KB_1 \models (P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1})$

**Step 3: After moving to [1,2] with Stench and no Breeze (2 marks)**
$KB_2 = KB_1 \cup \{S_{1,2}, \neg B_{1,2}\}$
Stench rule: $S_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow (W_{1,1} \vee W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2})$
With $\neg W_{1,1}$ → $KB_2 \models (W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2})$
No-breeze: $\neg B_{1,2} \Leftrightarrow \neg(P_{1,1} \vee P_{1,3} \vee P_{2,2})$
With $\neg P_{1,1}$ → $KB_2 \models (\neg P_{1,3} \wedge \neg P_{2,2})$

**Step 4: Combining and Resolving (1 mark)**
From $P_{2,2} \vee P_{3,1}$ and $\neg P_{2,2}$ → $KB_2 \models P_{3,1}$
From $W_{1,3} \vee W_{2,2}$ and $\neg W_{2,2}$ (inferred from $\neg S_{2,2}$ in the next step) → $W_{1,3}$.

**Step 5: Safety Marking (1 mark)**

| Cell | Status | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| [1,1] | **Safe** | Initial position, axiom |
| [1,2] | **Safe** (visited, no danger) | Direct percepts |
| [2,1] | **Safe** (visited) | Direct percepts |
| [2,2] | **Safe** | $\neg P_{2,2}$ and $\neg W_{2,2}$ (no stench at [1,2] implies no Wumpus at [2,2] or [1,3] or [1,1] — combined with $\neg W_{1,1}$) |
| [3,1] | **Pit** | $P_{3,1}$ proved by resolution |
| [1,3] | **Unsafe (Wumpus)** | $W_{1,3}$ proved by resolution |
| All other cells | **Unknown** | No information yet |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (CRITICAL):** Many students mistakenly mark a cell as safe just because it has *not yet been proved unsafe*. The correct statement is: a cell is **safe only if both** $\neg P_{x,y}$ **and** $\neg W_{x,y}$ are *proved*. A cell is **unsafe** if it has been *proved* to be a pit or contain the Wumpus. Anything else is **unknown** (or "possibly safe" / "possibly unsafe"), not "safe."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — KBAs and Wumpus World**

- **KBA = KB + Inference Engine + TELL/ASK + Percept Loop.**
- **Two approaches:** Declarative (Tell the *what*) vs Procedural (Tell the *how*).
- **Three levels:** Knowledge, Logical, Implementation.
- **Five good representation properties:** Representational adequacy, Inferential adequacy, Inferential efficiency, Learnability, Clarity.
- **Wumpus World is:** Partially Observable, Deterministic (in rules), Sequential, Static, Discrete, Single-Agent.
- **PEAS:**
  - **P**: +1000 (gold escape), −1000 (death), −1/action, −10/arrow.
  - **E**: 4×4 grid, start [1,1] facing East.
  - **A**: MoveForward, TurnLeft, TurnRight, Grab, Shoot, Climb.
  - **S**: Stench, Breeze, Glitter, Bump, Scream.
- **Key logical symbols:** $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow, \models, \vdash$.
- **Entailment is semantic; Inference is syntactic. Soundness + Completeness = perfect inference.**
- **Resolution Rule:** Combine clauses with complementary literals; eliminates one pair.
- **Modus Ponens:** From $\alpha \Rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha$, infer $\beta$.
- **Safety rule for Wumpus:** A cell is **safe** iff both $\neg P$ and $\neg W$ are *proved* in the KB.
- **Inference order in Wumpus:** Apply causal rules first → resolve disjunctions → use unit resolution → propagate derived facts.
- **Common exam trap:** Forgetting that [1,1] is *axiomatically safe* and that the *stench at a cell means Wumpus is in an ADJACENT cell, not the cell itself.*
- **Mnemonic for percepts:** **"S-B-G-B-S"** = **S**tench, **B**reeze, **G**litter, **B**ump, **S**cream.
- **Inference algorithms ranked by KTU weightage:** Modus Ponens (high) → Resolution (very high) → Forward/Backward Chaining (high) → Truth Table Enumeration (medium).
- **Sample exam one-liner:** "A knowledge-based agent is one that uses **symbolic reasoning** over an explicit KB to choose actions, distinguishing it from reflex agents that use condition–action rules."

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Knowledge, Logic, and Reasoning Patterns**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logic - Propositional Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAIT302 - FOUNDATIONS OF ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Logic - Propositional Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Russell & Norvig):** *Propositional Logic (PL)*, also called *Boolean Logic* or *Sentential Calculus*, is the simplest form of logic that represents facts about the world using **declarative sentences (propositions)** combined through **logical connectives**. Each proposition evaluates to exactly one of two truth values: **TRUE (T / 1)** or **FALSE (F / 0)**.

Formally, a proposition is a declarative statement that has a definite truth value and contains **no variables**. Propositional Logic is the formal study of reasoning where the atomic units of meaning are whole declarative statements.

The **Alphabet** of Propositional Logic consists of:
1. **Propositional Symbols:** $P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ — atomic, indivisible declarative statements.
2. **Logical Connectives:** $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ — the five standard operators.
3. **Constants:** $\text{True}$ and $\text{False}$.
4. **Parentheses:** $( , )$ — for grouping and disambiguation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** The five standard connectives are taught in a specific order of precedence: $\neg > \wedge > \vee > \rightarrow > \leftrightarrow$. This is a *high-yield* exam point because incorrect precedence is one of the most common errors in evaluation.

### 1.2 The Five Standard Logical Connectives

| Symbol | Name | Natural-Language Reading | Truth Value Rule |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| $\neg P$ | Negation / NOT | "It is not the case that P" | Flips truth value |
| $P \wedge Q$ | Conjunction / AND | "P and Q" | True **only** when both are True |
| $P \vee Q$ | Disjunction / OR | "P or Q" (inclusive) | False **only** when both are False |
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | Implication / IF-THEN | "If P then Q" | False **only** when P is True and Q is False |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | Biconditional / IFF | "P if and only if Q" | True when both have the **same** truth value |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Judge in a Courtroom"

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Strict Judge:** Imagine every proposition is a *witness* in a courtroom who must answer "Guilty (T)" or "Not Guilty (F)". The five connectives are the *rules of evidence* the judge applies.
> - **NOT ($\neg$):** A witness who always says the *opposite* of the truth.
> - **AND ($\wedge$):** A joint testimony — accepted as TRUE only if **both** witnesses are honest.
> - **OR ($\vee$):** A "either-or" testimony — at least one witness is telling the truth.
> - **IF-THEN ($\rightarrow$):** A *contract* — "If the suspect was at the scene, then the fingerprints are his." The contract is broken (FALSE) only when the antecedent is true but the consequent is false.
> - **IFF ($\leftrightarrow$):** A *two-way contract* — both directions must hold simultaneously.

This analogy clarifies why "$P \rightarrow Q$" is *vacuously true* when $P$ is False — a satisfied antecedent obligation that was never triggered is a perfectly valid contract.

### 1.4 Why Propositional Logic Matters in AI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Propositional Logic is the **backbone of digital circuit design (Boolean algebra)**, **knowledge representation in expert systems**, **automated theorem proving**, and **SAT solvers** (used in hardware verification, cryptography, and planning). In AI, it is the foundational layer upon which more expressive logics (First-Order Logic, Description Logic) are built.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Syntax vs. Semantics — The Two Pillars

The study of Propositional Logic has two distinct dimensions:

1. **Syntax (Form):** The rules governing how symbols are legally combined into well-formed formulas (WFFs).
2. **Semantics (Meaning):** The rules for assigning truth values to formulas via *interpretations* (also called *models*).

A **Well-Formed Formula (WFF)** in Propositional Logic is defined recursively:

- A propositional symbol $P$ alone is a WFF.
- The constants **True** and **False** are WFFs.
- If $\alpha$ is a WFF, then $\neg \alpha$ is a WFF.
- If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are WFFs, then $(\alpha \wedge \beta)$, $(\alpha \vee \beta)$, $(\alpha \rightarrow \beta)$, $(\alpha \leftrightarrow \beta)$ are WFFs.
- Nothing else is a WFF.

### 2.2 The Semantics: Truth Tables (Exhaustive)

An *interpretation* assigns a truth value to every propositional symbol. For $n$ distinct symbols, there are exactly $2^n$ possible interpretations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** This exponential blow-up is precisely why Propositional Logic becomes computationally hard for large knowledge bases. This motivates the need for efficient algorithms like **DPLL** and **CDCL** used in modern SAT solvers.

### 2.3 The Master Formula Sheet — Logical Equivalences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD CHEAT SHEET:** The following equivalence laws are *routinely* tested in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize them column-by-column.

**Table 1: Fundamental Logical Equivalences (use $\equiv$ for "is logically equivalent to")**

| Law | Equivalence 1 | Equivalence 2 |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Identity | $P \wedge \text{True} \equiv P$ | $P \vee \text{False} \equiv P$ |
| Domination | $P \vee \text{True} \equiv \text{True}$ | $P \wedge \text{False} \equiv \text{False}$ |
| Idempotent | $P \vee P \equiv P$ | $P \wedge P \equiv P$ |
| Double Negation | $\neg(\neg P) \equiv P$ | — |
| Commutative | $P \wedge Q \equiv Q \wedge P$ | $P \vee Q \equiv Q \vee P$ |
| Associative | $(P \wedge Q) \wedge R \equiv P \wedge (Q \wedge R)$ | $(P \vee Q) \vee R \equiv P \vee (Q \vee R)$ |
| Distributive | $P \wedge (Q \vee R) \equiv (P \wedge Q) \vee (P \wedge R)$ | $P \vee (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)$ |
| De Morgan's | $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$ | $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$ |
| Absorption | $P \vee (P \wedge Q) \equiv P$ | $P \wedge (P \vee Q) \equiv P$ |
| Negation | $P \vee \neg P \equiv \text{True}$ | $P \wedge \neg P \equiv \text{False}$ |

**Table 2: Implication and Biconditional Transformations (HIGHEST PRIORITY)**

| Original Form | Equivalent Form | KTU Significance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\neg P \vee Q$ | Eliminates $\rightarrow$ in CNF conversion |
| $P \rightarrow Q$ | $\neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$ | The *Contrapositive* (extremely testable) |
| $\neg(P \rightarrow Q)$ | $P \wedge \neg Q$ | Negated implication |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | $(P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$ | Expansion to two implications |
| $P \leftrightarrow Q$ | $(P \wedge Q) \vee (\neg P \wedge \neg Q)$ | Expansion for DNF |
| $\neg(P \leftrightarrow Q)$ | $(P \wedge \neg Q) \vee (\neg P \wedge Q)$ | Exclusive-OR (XOR) |

### 2.4 Classification of Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Universal Categories:** Every Propositional Logic formula falls into *exactly one* of these three categories — examiners love this classification.

- **Tautology (Valid):** A formula that is **True under every possible interpretation**. Example: $P \vee \neg P$ (Law of Excluded Middle).
- **Contradiction (Inconsistent / Unsatisfiable):** A formula that is **False under every interpretation**. Example: $P \wedge \neg P$.
- **Contingency (Satisfiable but not Valid):** A formula that is True under *some* interpretations and False under *others*. Example: $P \wedge Q$.

### 2.5 Normal Forms — The Standardized Representations

**Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF):** A conjunction (AND) of *clauses*, where each clause is a disjunction (OR) of *literals* (a literal is a propositional symbol or its negation).

$$ \text{CNF Schema:} \quad (L_{11} \vee L_{12} \vee \dots) \wedge (L_{21} \vee L_{22} \vee \dots) \wedge \dots $$

**Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF):** A disjunction (OR) of *terms*, where each term is a conjunction (AND) of literals.

$$ \text{DNF Schema:} \quad (L_{11} \wedge L_{12} \wedge \dots) \vee (L_{21} \wedge L_{22} \wedge \dots) \vee \dots $$

**Horn Clause:** A clause containing **at most one positive literal**. Example: $(\neg P \vee \neg Q \vee R)$. Horn clauses are critical because inference over Horn-form KBs is polynomial-time (used in **logic programming / Prolog**).

### 2.6 Logical Inference Rules

| Rule | Pattern | Form |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Modus Ponens** | From $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\alpha$, infer $\beta$ | $\dfrac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha}{\beta}$ |
| **Modus Tollens** | From $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ and $\neg \beta$, infer $\neg \alpha$ | $\dfrac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \neg \beta}{\neg \alpha}$ |
| **Resolution** | From $\alpha \vee \beta$ and $\neg \beta \vee \gamma$, infer $\alpha \vee \gamma$ | $\dfrac{\alpha \vee \beta, \quad \neg \beta \vee \gamma}{\alpha \vee \gamma}$ |
| **And-Elimination** | From $\alpha \wedge \beta$, infer $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{\alpha \wedge \beta}{\alpha}$ |
| **Unit Resolution** | Special case where one clause is a single literal | $\dfrac{\alpha \vee \beta, \quad \neg \beta}{\alpha}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Truth Table for $(P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \vee Q)$

This is the **canonical proof** that an implication is logically equivalent to a disjunction. It is a guaranteed Part B question at some point in the KTU cycle.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of distinct propositional symbols.**
There are $2$ symbols ($P$ and $Q$), so the truth table must have $2^2 = 4$ rows.

**Step 2 — Enumerate all $2^n = 4$ interpretations.**
List all combinations of truth values for $P$ and $Q$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\neg P$ and $\neg Q$ columns first** (lowest precedence, depends on one variable).

**Step 4 — Compute $P \rightarrow Q$** using the rule: False only when P=T and Q=F.

**Step 5 — Compute $\neg P \vee Q$** using the rule: True when at least one operand is True.

**Step 6 — Compute the final biconditional $\leftrightarrow$** by checking equality of the two sub-formulas.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Row 1: } & P = T, \ Q = T \\
& \neg P = F, \quad \neg Q = F \\
& P \rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(antecedent T, consequent T)} \\
& \neg P \vee Q = F \vee T = T \\
& (P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \vee Q) = T \leftrightarrow T = \mathbf{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Row 2: } & P = T, \ Q = F \\
& \neg P = F, \quad \neg Q = T \\
& P \rightarrow Q = F \quad \text{(antecedent T, consequent F — contract broken)} \\
& \neg P \vee Q = F \vee F = F \\
& (P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \vee Q) = F \leftrightarrow F = \mathbf{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Row 3: } & P = F, \ Q = T \\
& \neg P = T, \quad \neg Q = F \\
& P \rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(antecedent F — vacuously true)} \\
& \neg P \vee Q = T \vee T = T \\
& (P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \vee Q) = T \leftrightarrow T = \mathbf{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Row 4: } & P = F, \ Q = F \\
& \neg P = T, \quad \neg Q = T \\
& P \rightarrow Q = T \quad \text{(antecedent F — vacuously true)} \\
& \neg P \vee Q = T \vee F = T \\
& (P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (\neg P \vee Q) = T \leftrightarrow T = \mathbf{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the final column contains all **T** values across all 4 rows, the formula is a **TAUTOLOGY**. This proves the equivalence $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$ for all interpretations.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Converting $(P \rightarrow Q) \leftrightarrow (Q \rightarrow P)$ to CNF

**Step 1 — Eliminate the biconditional** using $A \leftrightarrow B \equiv (A \rightarrow B) \wedge (B \rightarrow A)$:

$$ \big[ (P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow P) \big] \wedge \big[ (Q \rightarrow P) \rightarrow (P \rightarrow Q) \big] $$

**Step 2 — Eliminate the outer implications** using $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$:

$$ \big[ \neg(P \rightarrow Q) \vee (Q \rightarrow P) \big] \wedge \big[ \neg(Q \rightarrow P) \vee (P \rightarrow Q) \big] $$

**Step 3 — Eliminate the inner implications** using the same rule on $P \rightarrow Q$ and $Q \rightarrow P$:

$$ \big[ \neg(\neg P \vee Q) \vee (\neg Q \vee P) \big] \wedge \big[ \neg(\neg Q \vee P) \vee (\neg P \vee Q) \big] $$

**Step 4 — Apply De Morgan's Law** to the negated compound terms:

$$ \big[ (\neg\neg P \wedge \neg Q) \vee (\neg Q \vee P) \big] \wedge \big[ (\neg\neg Q \wedge \neg P) \vee (\neg P \vee Q) \big] $$

**Step 5 — Apply Double Negation** to simplify $\neg\neg P$ and $\neg\neg Q$:

$$ \big[ (P \wedge \neg Q) \vee (\neg Q \vee P) \big] \wedge \big[ (Q \wedge \neg P) \vee (\neg P \vee Q) \big] $$

**Step 6 — Apply Distributive Law** $A \wedge (B \vee C) \equiv (A \wedge B) \vee (A \wedge C)$ inside each big disjunction:

$$ \big[ (P \vee \neg Q \vee P) \wedge (\neg Q \vee \neg Q \vee P) \big] \wedge \big[ (Q \vee \neg P \vee Q) \wedge (\neg P \vee \neg P \vee Q) \big] $$

**Step 7 — Apply Idempotence** ($P \vee P \equiv P$) to clean up:

$$ \big[ (P \vee \neg Q) \wedge (\neg Q \vee P) \big] \wedge \big[ (Q \vee \neg P) \wedge (\neg P \vee Q) \big] $$

**Final CNF:**

$$ (P \vee \neg Q) \wedge (Q \vee \neg P) $$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Validity of Resolution Rule

We must prove that the resolution inference rule is **sound**, i.e., if premises are true, the conclusion is necessarily true.

**Premise 1:** $\alpha \vee \beta$ is True.
**Premise 2:** $\neg \beta \vee \gamma$ is True.
**Conclusion:** $\alpha \vee \gamma$ must be True.

**Case Analysis on $\beta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Case 1: } & \beta = T \\
& \text{From Premise 2, since } \neg \beta = F, \text{ we require } \gamma = T. \\
& \text{Therefore } \alpha \vee \gamma = \alpha \vee T = T. \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Case 2: } & \beta = F \\
& \text{From Premise 1, since } \beta = F, \text{ we require } \alpha = T. \\
& \text{Therefore } \alpha \vee \gamma = T \vee \gamma = T. \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** In all cases, $\alpha \vee \gamma$ is True. Hence resolution is a **sound inference rule**. Moreover, resolution is also **complete** for Propositional Logic (any entailed clause can be derived by resolution alone — this is the foundation of automated theorem provers).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Inference Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this decision matrix in exam situations to pick the right tool:**

| Goal | Method to Use | Complexity | When Applicable |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Check if a single formula is a tautology | Truth Table | $O(2^n)$ | Small $n$ (≤ 5) |
| Check if KB entails $\alpha$ | Model Checking (TT) | $O(2^n)$ | Small KB |
| Prove entailment by inference | Forward / Backward Chaining | Polynomial | **Horn clauses only** |
| Prove entailment for general KB | Resolution Refutation | Exponential worst-case | General clauses |
| Check satisfiability | DPLL / WalkSAT | NP-complete | General SAT |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture: The Hierarchy of Propositional Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Propositional Logic System"] --> B["Syntax Layer"]
    A --> C["Semantics Layer"]
    A --> D["Inference Layer"]
    A --> E["Application Layer"]
    B --> B1["Alphabet Symbols"]
    B --> B2["Logical Connectives"]
    B --> B3["Well Formed Formulas WFF"]
    B --> B4["Precedence Rules"]
    C --> C1["Interpretations or Models"]
    C --> C2["Truth Tables"]
    C --> C3["Tautology Contradiction Contingency"]
    C --> C4["Equivalence and Entailment"]
    D --> D1["Modus Ponens"]
    D --> D2["Modus Tollens"]
    D --> D3["Resolution Rule"]
    D --> D4["Forward and Backward Chaining"]
    E --> E1["Digital Circuit Design"]
    E --> E2["SAT Solvers"]
    E --> E3["Expert Systems KB"]
    E --> E4["Automated Planning"]
    B1 --> F["Atomic Foundation"]
    B2 --> F
    C1 --> G["Meaning and Truth"]
    C2 --> G
    D1 --> H["Reasoning Engine"]
    D3 --> H
    E2 --> I["Real World Deployment"]
    E3 --> I
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Resolution Refutation Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Knowledge Base KB and Query alpha"]) --> Step1["Step 1: Negate the query to get NOT alpha"]
    Step1 --> Step2["Step 2: Convert KB AND NOT alpha to CNF"]
    Step2 --> Step3["Step 3: Initialize clause set S with CNF clauses"]
    Step3 --> LoopCheck{"Are there two clauses containing complementary literals?"}
    LoopCheck --> Step4["Step 4: Pick two such clauses and apply Resolution"]
    Step4 --> Step5["Step 5: Add resolvent clause to S"]
    Step5 --> ConflictCheck{"Did S contain empty clause?"}
    LoopCheck --> NoNew["No new resolvents possible"] --> Unsat["KB does NOT entail alpha"]
    ConflictCheck --> Yes["Yes empty clause derived"] --> Sat["KB entails alpha PROVED"]
    ConflictCheck --> NoEmpty["No empty clause yet"] --> LoopCheck
```

### 4.3 Logical Map: Connective Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["P antecedent"] --> Imply["Implies P rightarrow Q"]
    Q["Q consequent"] --> Imply
    Imply --> Contract1["Negation of P or Q"]
    Contract1 --> OrForm["NOT P OR Q"]
    OrForm --> DeMorgan["De Morgan Inverts AND to OR"]
    DeMorgan --> CnfForm["Standard CNF form"]
    Imply --> Contra["Contrapositive NOT Q rightarrow NOT P"]
    Contra --> Equiv["Logically Equivalent"]
    OrForm --> Equiv
    Imply --> ModusP["Modus Ponens extracts Q"]
    P --> ModusP
    OrForm --> ModusP
    CnfForm --> SatApp["Feeds SAT Solver"]
    Equiv --> TruthTab["Verified by Truth Table"]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Formula Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Given Propositional Formula phi"] --> Count["Count number of interpretations n equals 2 to power k"]
    Count --> Build["Build truth table for all rows"]
    Build --> Check1{"All rows True?"}
    Check1 --> Yes1["YES - phi is TAUTOLOGY valid"]
    Check1 --> No1["NO - check next condition"]
    No1 --> Check2{"All rows False?"}
    Check2 --> Yes2["YES - phi is CONTRADICTION unsatisfiable"]
    Check2 --> No2["NO - phi is CONTINGENCY satisfiable but not valid"]
    Yes1 --> End1["Result: Valid Knowledge"]
    Yes2 --> End2["Result: Unsatisfiable Knowledge Base"]
    No2 --> End3["Result: Mixed - Search Models"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define a *tautology*, *contradiction*, and *contingency* in Propositional Logic. Give one example for each.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Tautology:** A formula that evaluates to **True under every possible interpretation**. The Law of Excluded Middle $P \vee \neg P$ is the classic example. Equivalently, the implication $P \rightarrow P$ is always true.
>
> **Contradiction:** A formula that evaluates to **False under every interpretation**. The minimum example is $P \wedge \neg P$. A contradiction means the formula is *unsatisfiable* and cannot be a meaningful knowledge base.
>
> **Contingency:** A formula that is **True for some interpretations and False for others**. Example: $P \wedge Q$ is True only when both P and Q are True (1 out of 4 rows).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** State and prove De Morgan's Laws in Propositional Logic using truth tables.

**Model Answer (Truth Table for $\neg(P \wedge Q) \equiv \neg P \vee \neg Q$):**

| $P$ | $Q$ | $P \wedge Q$ | $\neg(P \wedge Q)$ | $\neg P$ | $\neg Q$ | $\neg P \vee \neg Q$ | Equivalence |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | F | F | F | F | **T** |
| T | F | F | T | F | T | T | **T** |
| F | T | F | T | T | F | T | **T** |
| F | F | F | T | T | T | T | **T** |

Since columns 4 and 7 match exactly, the equivalence holds. The dual law $\neg(P \vee Q) \equiv \neg P \wedge \neg Q$ is proved similarly.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Convert the following formula into Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) and identify whether it is a **Horn clause**:

$$ (P \rightarrow Q) \rightarrow (\neg R \wedge P) $$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Eliminate the outer implication** using $A \rightarrow B \equiv \neg A \vee B$:

$$ \neg(P \rightarrow Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge P) $$

**Step 2 — Eliminate the inner implication** $P \rightarrow Q$:

$$ \neg(\neg P \vee Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge P) $$

**Step 3 — Apply De Morgan's Law** to the negated disjunction:

$$ (P \wedge \neg Q) \vee (\neg R \wedge P) $$

**Step 4 — Apply the Distributive Law** to factor out the common $P$:

$$ P \wedge (\neg Q \vee \neg R) $$

**Step 5 — Final CNF Form:**

$$ \boxed{\,P \,\wedge\, (\neg Q \vee \neg R)\,} $$

**Step 6 — Horn Clause Check:**
- Clause 1: $P$ contains **one positive literal** — Horn ✓
- Clause 2: $\neg Q \vee \neg R$ contains **zero positive literals** — Horn ✓

> **Conclusion:** Both clauses are Horn clauses, so the entire CNF is a **conjunction of Horn clauses**, hence it represents a **Horn formula**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Test the validity of the following argument using the **truth table method**:

$$ \text{Premise 1: } P \rightarrow Q, \quad \text{Premise 2: } Q \rightarrow R, \quad \text{Conclusion: } P \rightarrow R $$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

This argument is **valid** if the conclusion is true in *every row* where both premises are true. Build a 3-variable table ($2^3 = 8$ rows):

| $P$ | $Q$ | $R$ | $P \rightarrow Q$ | $Q \rightarrow R$ | $P \rightarrow R$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | T | T | T |
| T | T | F | T | F | F |
| T | F | T | F | T | T |
| T | F | F | F | T | F |
| F | T | T | T | T | T |
| F | T | F | T | F | T |
| F | F | T | T | T | T |
| F | F | F | T | T | T |

**Step 1 — Identify rows where BOTH premises are TRUE:** Rows 1, 5, 7, 8.

**Step 2 — Check the conclusion in those rows:**
- Row 1: $P \rightarrow R = T$ ✓
- Row 5: $P \rightarrow R = T$ ✓
- Row 7: $P \rightarrow R = T$ ✓
- Row 8: $P \rightarrow R = T$ ✓

> **Conclusion:** In every row where both premises are True, the conclusion is also True. Therefore the argument is **logically VALID** (this is the famous **Hypothetical Syllogism**).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the **Resolution Refutation** method to prove that the following KB entails $R$:

$$ \text{KB: } \{ P \vee Q, \quad \neg P \vee R, \quad \neg Q \vee R \} $$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Negate the query** $R$ to get $\neg R$.

**Step 2 — Combine KB with negated query** and write all clauses:
- Clause 1: $P \vee Q$
- Clause 2: $\neg P \vee R$
- Clause 3: $\neg Q \vee R$
- Clause 4: $\neg R$ (negated goal)

**Step 3 — Resolve Clause 2 and Clause 4** on complementary literals $R$ and $\neg R$:

$$ \text{Resolvent:} \quad \neg P $$

**Step 4 — Resolve Clause 3 and Clause 4** on $R$ and $\neg R$:

$$ \text{Resolvent:} \quad \neg Q $$

**Step 5 — Resolve Clause 1 with $\neg P$** on $P$:

$$ \text{Resolvent:} \quad Q $$

**Step 6 — Resolve $Q$ with $\neg Q$**:

$$ \text{Resolvent:} \quad \square \quad \text{(empty clause)} $$

> **Conclusion:** The derivation of the **empty clause (contradiction)** proves that $KB \cup \{\neg R\}$ is unsatisfiable, therefore $KB \models R$. **Q.E.D.**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Modus Ponens** and **Modus Tollens** inference rules with truth table verification.

**Model Answer:**

**Modus Ponens (Affirming the Antecedent):**
- **Pattern:** $\dfrac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \alpha}{\beta}$
- **Logical Form:** If $\alpha$ then $\beta$. $\alpha$ is true. Therefore $\beta$ is true.
- **Example:** If it rains, the ground is wet. It is raining. Therefore the ground is wet.

**Truth Table Verification:**

| $\alpha$ | $\beta$ | $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ | Premises True? | $\beta$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| T | T | T | YES | **T** ✓ |
| T | F | F | NO | — |
| F | T | T | NO | — |
| F | F | T | NO | — |

The only row where the premise $\alpha \rightarrow \beta$ is true AND $\alpha$ is true is Row 1, where $\beta = T$. Hence the rule is **sound**.

**Modus Tollens (Denying the Consequent):**
- **Pattern:** $\dfrac{\alpha \rightarrow \beta, \quad \neg \beta}{\neg \alpha}$
- **Logical Form:** If $\alpha$ then $\beta$. $\beta$ is false. Therefore $\alpha$ is false.
- **Example:** If the switch is on, the bulb glows. The bulb is not glowing. Therefore the switch is not on.

**Truth Table Verification:** The only row where $\alpha \rightarrow \beta = T$ AND $\neg \beta = T$ is when $\alpha = F$ and $\beta = F$. Hence $\neg \alpha = T$ is correctly derived.

> **Conclusion:** Both rules are **sound** inference rules and form the basis of forward and backward chaining algorithms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Implication Misconception:** A common error is writing $P \rightarrow Q \equiv P \wedge Q$. This is **wrong** — implication is equivalent to $\neg P \vee Q$.
> 2. **CNF Conversion Mistake:** Students often stop at eliminating implications but forget to apply De Morgan's Law to push negations **inward** (NNF step).
> 3. **Precedence Errors:** Writing $P \wedge Q \vee R$ ambiguously. Always use parentheses: either $(P \wedge Q) \vee R$ or $P \wedge (Q \vee R)$.
> 4. **Resolution Without Negation:** To prove $KB \models \alpha$ via resolution, you must add **$\neg \alpha$** to the KB. Forgetting this step invalidates the entire proof.
> 5. **Vacuous Truth Confusion:** Students often incorrectly evaluate $P \rightarrow Q$ as False when $P$ is False. Remember: it is **vacuously TRUE** in that case.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Propositional Logic**

- **Propositions** are declarative statements with definite truth values (T/F).
- **Five Connectives:** $\neg, \wedge, \vee, \rightarrow, \leftrightarrow$ — in order of decreasing precedence.
- **Implication Rule:** $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg P \vee Q$; only False when $P = T, Q = F$.
- **Contrapositive:** $P \rightarrow Q \equiv \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$ (the most-asked identity).
- **De Morgan's Laws:** Push negation inward by flipping the connective.
- **Distributive Law:** $A \wedge (B \vee C) \equiv (A \wedge B) \vee (A \wedge C)$ — key for CNF/DNF conversion.
- **Three Categories:** Tautology (always T), Contradiction (always F), Contingency (mixed).
- **CNF = AND of ORs**, **DNF = OR of ANDs** — both are universal canonical forms.
- **Horn Clause:** At most **one positive literal** per clause — supports polynomial inference.
- **Modus Ponens & Modus Tollens** are the two primary forward-chaining rules.
- **Resolution** is the single most powerful rule — it is both **sound and complete** for PL.
- **Resolution Refutation** is the standard automated theorem-proving technique: add $\neg \alpha$ and derive the empty clause $\square$.
- **SAT Problem:** Checking satisfiability of a CNF formula is **NP-complete** — but DPLL/WalkSAT handle large instances efficiently in practice.
- **Vacuously True:** Any implication with a False antecedent is automatically True.
- **Soundness** = derived conclusions are always true. **Completeness** = all true conclusions can be derived.

---

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition of First-Order Logic (FOL)

**First-Order Logic (FOL)**, also called **First-Order Predicate Logic (FOPL)** or **Predicate Calculus**, is a powerful representational language used in Artificial Intelligence (AI) to express facts about objects, their properties, and the relationships between them. Unlike Propositional Logic, which only deals with declarative statements that are either true or false, FOL adds **quantifiers**, **variables**, **predicates**, and **functions**, making it possible to express general rules, infinite domains, and complex relational structures with mathematical precision.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> First-Order Logic is a representational language whose syntactic richness allows it to express properties of entire collections of objects rather than being limited to individual propositions. It is the foundation of **Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KRR)** in AI systems.

The formal structure of FOL rests on a vocabulary consisting of:
- **Logical symbols** (with fixed meaning): $\land, \lor, \neg, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow, \forall, \exists, =, (, ), ,$
- **Non-logical symbols** (domain-specific): constants, predicate symbols, function symbols
- **Variables**: $x, y, z, a, b, c, \ldots$

A **model** in FOL is a pair $M = (D, I)$, where $D$ is a non-empty set called the **domain of discourse** (the universe of objects we wish to talk about), and $I$ is an **interpretation function** that maps non-logical symbols to their referents in $D$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Photo Album" Analogy

Imagine you are building a **family photo album** that needs to be searchable by rules:

- **Propositional Logic** is like writing sticky notes on each photo: "Photo-1 has a man", "Photo-2 has a child", "Photo-3 has a woman". You cannot ask: "Is there a photo of someone's grandmother?" — there is no way to express *relationship chains*.
- **First-Order Logic** is like writing a **genealogy database**: you define predicates like `Mother(x, y)`, `Parent(x, y)`, and then you can ask:
  - $\forall x \forall y \ (Mother(x,y) \Rightarrow Parent(x,y))$ — "Every mother is a parent" (universally true).
  - $\exists x \ (Mother(x, \text{Mary}) \land \text{Female}(x))$ — "There exists a female who is Mary's mother."

This **expressiveness** is exactly why FOL is the backbone of AI knowledge representation — it allows **infinite truths** in a finite knowledge base.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students**
> FOL is **semi-decidable**: it is possible to prove every true sentence, but it is **not decidable** — one cannot always determine whether a sentence is true or false in finite time. This is a critical theoretical limitation.

## 1.3 Why FOL Matters in AI Engineering

FOL is foundational for:
- **Expert Systems** (e.g., MYCIN, DENDRAL)
- **Semantic Web & Ontologies** (RDF, OWL)
- **Automated Theorem Provers** (Prover9, Lean, Coq)
- **Natural Language Understanding** (semantic parsing)
- **Database Query Languages** (SQL has FOL semantics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher-Order Logic (HOL)** allows quantification over predicates themselves (e.g., $\forall P \ P(x)$), while FOL restricts quantification to **objects only**. HOL is strictly more expressive but is even less tractable computationally.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Syntactic Components of FOL — The Building Blocks

A well-formed formula (WFF) in FOL is constructed using the following vocabulary:

### 2.1.1 Symbols

| Symbol Type | Notation | Examples | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Constant Symbols** | $a, b, c, \ldots$ | KingJohn, 0, 2, Richard | Represent specific objects in the domain |
| **Variable Symbols** | $x, y, z, a, b$ | $x, y, z$ | Range over objects in the domain |
| **Predicate Symbols** | $P, Q, R, \ldots$ | Brother, Father, $>$, Even | Represent relations; $n$-ary predicate takes $n$ terms |
| **Function Symbols** | $f, g, h, \ldots$ | Sqrt, LegOf, Plus, FatherOf | Map tuples of objects to a unique object |
| **Logical Connectives** | $\land, \lor, \neg, \Rightarrow, \Leftrightarrow$ | — | Standard Boolean operators |
| **Quantifiers** | $\forall, \exists$ | — | Universal and Existential |
| **Equality** | $=$ | — | Identity relation |
| **Punctuation** | $(, ), ,$ | — | Disambiguation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Terms** in FOL are defined recursively:
> 1. A constant symbol is a term.
> 2. A variable symbol is a term.
> 3. If $f$ is an $n$-ary function symbol and $t_1, \ldots, t_n$ are terms, then $f(t_1, \ldots, t_n)$ is a term.

### 2.1.2 Atomic and Complex Sentences

**Atomic Sentence** (the smallest meaningful unit):
- $P(t_1, t_2, \ldots, t_n)$ — a predicate applied to $n$ terms
- $t_1 = t_2$ — an equality assertion
- **Example:** $Brother(Richard, John)$, $Even(Sqrt(4))$, $2 < 4$

**Complex Sentence**: Built by combining atomic sentences using logical connectives.
- **Example:** $\neg Brother(Richard, John) \land (King(John) \Rightarrow Rich(John))$

### 2.1.3 Quantifier Scope and Bound vs. Free Variables

A quantifier $\forall x$ or $\exists x$ **binds** every occurrence of $x$ in the formula that follows it (within its **scope**). Variables not bound by any quantifier are called **free variables**.

> [!WARNING]
> **A sentence with free variables is not a well-formed closed sentence in FOL.** KTU examiners often ask: "Is the formula $\forall x P(x, y)$ a sentence?" — the answer is **NO** because $y$ is free.

## 2.2 Semantics of FOL — Truth in a Model

A **model** $M = (D, I)$ interprets a FOL sentence as true or false.

**Interpretation Function $I$** maps:
- Each constant $c \to$ an element $c^I \in D$
- Each $n$-ary predicate $P \to$ an $n$-ary relation $P^I \subseteq D^n$
- Each $n$-ary function $f \to$ an $n$-ary function $f^I : D^n \to D$
- Each variable $x \to$ an element in $D$ (a variable assignment)

### 2.2.1 Truth Conditions for Quantifiers

For any formula $P$ and model $M = (D, I)$ with variable assignment $\mu$:

$$M \models_{\mu} \forall x \, \phi \quad \iff \quad \text{for every } d \in D, \ M \models_{\mu[x/d]} \phi$$

$$M \models_{\mu} \exists x \, \phi \quad \iff \quad \text{there exists } d \in D \text{ such that } M \models_{\mu[x/d]} \phi$$

where $\mu[x/d]$ is the assignment identical to $\mu$ except that $x$ is mapped to $d$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU 2024 ESE** — these are the most frequently tested equivalences.

| Law / Rule | Logical Equivalence | Engineering Intuition |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantifier Negation (De Morgan for Quantifiers)** | $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$ | "Not everyone is rich" means "Someone is not rich" |
| **Quantifier Negation (Dual)** | $\neg \exists x \, P(x) \equiv \forall x \, \neg P(x)$ | "There is no perfect AI" means "All AIs are imperfect" |
| **Quantifier Distribution over $\land$** | $\forall x \, (P(x) \land Q(x)) \equiv \forall x \, P(x) \land \forall x \, Q(x)$ | Universally conjunction-distributive |
| **Quantifier Distribution over $\lor$** | $\exists x \, (P(x) \lor Q(x)) \equiv \exists x \, P(x) \lor \exists x \, Q(x)$ | Existentially disjunction-distributive |
| **Mixed Distribution (Universal over $\lor$)** | $\forall x \, (P(x) \lor Q) \equiv \forall x \, P(x) \lor Q$ (if $x \notin Q$) | Constants can be pulled out |
| **Mixed Distribution (Existential over $\land$)** | $\exists x \, (P(x) \land Q) \equiv \exists x \, P(x) \land Q$ (if $x \notin Q$) | Constants can be pulled out |
| **Quantifier Swap (¬ mixed)** | $\neg \exists x \forall y \, P(x,y) \equiv \forall x \exists y \, \neg P(x,y)$ | Critical for proof conversions |
| **Equivalence** | $P \Leftrightarrow Q \equiv (P \Rightarrow Q) \land (Q \Rightarrow P)$ | Used heavily in KB encoding |
| **Modus Ponens** | From $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $P$, infer $Q$ | Backbone of forward chaining |
| **Modus Tollens** | From $P \Rightarrow Q$ and $\neg Q$, infer $\neg P$ | Backbone of backward chaining |
| **Resolution** | $\frac{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_n}{\text{resolvent}}$ where $\ell_i, m_j$ are complementary | Complete for FOL after CNF conversion |
| **Unification** | $\text{UNIFY}(p, q) = \theta$ such that $\text{SUBST}(\theta, p) = \text{SUBST}(\theta, q)$ | Most General Unifier (MGU) is unique up to renaming |

### 2.3.1 Critical Quantifier Exchange Rules

$$\forall x \forall y \, P(x,y) \equiv \forall y \forall x \, P(x,y)$$

$$\exists x \exists y \, P(x,y) \equiv \exists y \exists x \, P(x,y)$$

**BUT** in general: $\forall x \exists y \, P(x,y) \not\equiv \exists y \forall x \, P(x,y)$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap**: $\forall x \exists y$ is **NOT** equivalent to $\exists y \forall x$. Example: "Every person has a mother" ($\forall x \exists y$) is **TRUE**, but "There is one mother for all" ($\exists y \forall x$) is **FALSE** in general. Examiners love this distinction.

## 2.4 Using FOL — The Wumpus World Example (from Russell & Norvig)

In the Wumpus World, the agent can use FOL to reason about its environment. The KB for Wumpus World might include rules such as:

- $\forall x \forall y \forall z \ Big(x, y) \land Pit(x, y) \land Adj(x, y, z) \Rightarrow Breeze(z)$
- $\forall x \forall y \ Big(x, y) \land \neg Breeze(x, y) \Rightarrow \neg Pit(x, y)$ (a derived rule by the agent)

The agent uses **logical inference** to deduce new facts (e.g., a square is safe) from sensory observations and rules.

## 2.5 Knowledge Engineering in FOL — The 7-Step Process

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Knowledge Engineering is the practical discipline of building a knowledge base. The process is:

1. **Identify the task** — Decide what queries the KB must support.
2. **Assemble the relevant knowledge** — Consult domain experts and gather facts.
3. **Decide on a vocabulary** — Choose predicates, functions, and constants.
4. **Encode general knowledge about the domain** — Axioms in FOL.
5. **Encode a description of the specific problem instance** — Assertions about the current state.
6. **Pose queries to the inference procedure and get answers** — Run the reasoner.
7. **Debug and extend the knowledge base** — Refine, test, and validate.

## 2.6 Inference in FOL

### 2.6.1 Reduction to Propositional Inference

One approach (used in early AI systems) is to:
1. Convert FOL KB to a propositional KB by **grounding** (substituting all variable combinations with constant tuples).
2. Apply propositional inference (e.g., DPLL, WalkSAT).

**Problem**: If the domain has $k$ symbols and $n$ constants, the grounded KB can have $O(k^n)$ sentences — **infinite** for infinite domains. Thus, propositionalization is **not efficient** in general.

### 2.6.2 Unification

**Unification** is the algorithm that finds a substitution $\theta$ making two logical expressions identical.

**Definition**: $\text{UNIFY}(\alpha, \beta) = \theta$ where $\text{SUBST}(\theta, \alpha) = \text{SUBST}(\theta, \beta)$.

A **Most General Unifier (MGU)** is the simplest such $\theta$ (no unnecessary bindings).

### 2.6.3 Generalized Modus Ponens (GMP)

GMP combines unification with modus ponens for variables:

$$\text{For } p_i', p_i \text{ where } \text{UNIFY}(p_i, p_i') = \theta_i:$$

$$\frac{p_1', \ p_2', \ldots, p_n', \quad (p_1 \land p_2 \land \cdots \land p_n \Rightarrow q)}{\text{SUBST}(\theta_1 \cup \theta_2 \cup \cdots \cup \theta_n, q)}$$

**Example**: From the rule $King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)$, $King(John)$, and $Greedy(y)$, we can conclude $Evil(John)$ using MGU $\theta = \{x/John, y/John\}$.

### 2.6.4 Forward Chaining (Data-Driven)

Starts from known facts and applies rules to derive new facts until goal is reached.
- **Sound**, **complete** for definite clauses
- **Efficient** when goal is unknown
- Used in: **production systems**, **deductive databases**

### 2.6.5 Backward Chaining (Goal-Driven)

Starts from a goal query and works backward, finding rules whose conclusion matches the goal.
- **Sound**, **complete** for definite clauses
- **Efficient** when the goal is known
- Used in: **logic programming** (e.g., **Prolog**)

### 2.6.6 Resolution Theorem Proving

The **resolution rule** for FOL (after CNF conversion) is the foundation of automated theorem proving:

$$\frac{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k, \quad m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_n}{\ell_1 \lor \cdots \lor \ell_{i-1} \lor \ell_{i+1} \lor \cdots \lor \ell_k \lor m_1 \lor \cdots \lor m_{j-1} \lor m_{j+1} \lor \cdots \lor m_n}$$

where $\text{UNIFY}(\ell_i, \neg m_j) = \theta$ and the result is the resolvent after applying $\theta$.

Resolution is **refutation-complete**: if a sentence $\alpha$ is entailed by a KB, resolution will eventually derive the **empty clause** $\square$ from $\text{KB} \land \neg \alpha$.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantifier Negation Equivalences

We derive the de Morgan-like laws for quantifiers using formal semantics.

**Theorem**: $\neg \forall x \, P(x) \equiv \exists x \, \neg P(x)$.

**Proof**:

$$\begin{aligned}
M \models \neg \forall x \, P(x) &\iff \text{it is not the case that } (M \models \forall x \, P(x)) \\
&\iff \text{it is not the case that } (\text{for all } d \in D, \, M \models P(d)) \\
&\iff \text{there exists } d \in D \text{ such that } M \not\models P(d) \\
&\iff \text{there exists } d \in D \text{ such that } M \models \neg P(d) \\
&\iff M \models \exists x \, \neg P(x)
\end{aligned}$$

This completes the proof. Each step is justified by the semantic definition of $\neg$, $\forall$, and $\exists$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Explanation**: "Not all swans are white" is logically the same as "There exists at least one swan that is not white." The same logic applies to the dual $\neg \exists \equiv \forall \neg$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Unification Algorithm

**Problem**: Find the MGU of $p = Knows(John, x)$ and $q = Knows(y, z)$.

**Step-by-step execution**:

| Step | Expression 1 | Expression 2 | Substitution | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Knows(John, x)$ | $Knows(y, z)$ | $\theta_1 = \emptyset$ | Init: both predicate heads match |
| 2 | $John$ | $y$ | $\theta_1 = \{y/John\}$ | $John$ is a constant, $y$ is a variable → bind $y$ to $John$ |
| 3 | $x$ | $z$ | $\theta_1 = \{y/John, z/x\}$ | $x$ and $z$ are both variables → bind one to the other |
| 4 | — | — | $\theta = \{y/John, z/x\}$ | MGU found; applied result: $Knows(John, x)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification**: $\text{SUBST}(\theta, p) = Knows(John, x)$ and $\text{SUBST}(\theta, q) = Knows(John, x)$. Both expressions are now identical. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example: Generalized Modus Ponens Application

**Given Rule**: $\forall x \, (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$

**Given Facts**: $King(John)$, $Greedy(John)$

**Step 1**: Eliminate universal quantifier (implicit in Prolog-style). Rule becomes:
$$King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x)$$

**Step 2**: Find a substitution $\theta$ such that the premises of the rule unify with the facts.
- Unify $King(x)$ with $King(John)$ → $\theta_1 = \{x/John\}$
- Unify $Greedy(x)$ with $Greedy(John)$ → $\theta_2 = \{x/John\}$

**Step 3**: Apply combined substitution to the conclusion $Evil(x)$:
$$\text{SUBST}(\{x/John\}, Evil(x)) = Evil(John)$$

**Conclusion**: $Evil(John)$ is derivable.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Prolog**, this is written as: `evil(X) :- king(X), greedy(X).` and the query `?- evil(john).` succeeds if `king(john)` and `greedy(john)` are true.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Converting FOL to CNF for Resolution

**Original Sentence**: $\forall x \, (King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x))$

**Step 1 — Eliminate implications**:
$$King(x) \land Greedy(x) \Rightarrow Evil(x) \equiv \neg(King(x) \land Greedy(x)) \lor Evil(x)$$

**Step 2 — Move $\neg$ inward (De Morgan)**:
$$\neg King(x) \lor \neg Greedy(x) \lor Evil(x)$$

**Step 3 — Standardize variables** (rename $x$ in each sub-formula — already done here).

**Step 4 — Skolemize** (no $\exists$ to remove — already in prenex form).

**Step 5 — Drop universal quantifiers** (implicit in CNF):
$$\neg King(x) \lor \neg Greedy(x) \lor Evil(x)$$

**Final CNF Clause**: $\{\neg King(x), \neg Greedy(x), Evil(x)\}$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Knowledge Engineering for "Electric Circuits"

**Step 1 — Task**: Diagnose faulty circuits using logical reasoning.

**Step 2 — Knowledge Assembly**: From an electrical engineer, learn that:
- In a series circuit, both switches must be ON for the bulb to light.
- In a parallel circuit, at least one switch must be ON.

**Step 3 — Vocabulary**:
- Constants: $S_1, S_2, B$ (switches 1, 2 and the bulb)
- Predicates: $On(x)$, $Lit(b)$, $Series(c)$, $Parallel(c)$

**Step 4 — General Domain Axioms**:
$$\forall c \, (Series(c) \land On(S_1) \land On(S_2) \Rightarrow Lit(B))$$

$$\forall c \, (Parallel(c) \land (On(S_1) \lor On(S_2)) \Rightarrow Lit(B))$$

**Step 5 — Specific Instance**:
- $Series(C_1)$, $On(S_1)$, $\neg On(S_2)$

**Step 6 — Query**: $Lit(B)$?

**Step 7 — Inference**: The query $\neg Lit(B)$ is added to the KB and resolution proceeds:
- From $\neg Lit(B)$ and the series rule, resolve to get $\neg Series(C_1) \lor \neg On(S_1) \lor \neg On(S_2)$
- This resolves with $Series(C_1)$ to yield $\neg On(S_1) \lor \neg On(S_2)$
- Resolves with $On(S_1)$ to yield $\neg On(S_2)$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: We have successfully proven $\neg On(S_2)$ — confirming our KB correctly identifies that the second switch is OFF.

## 3.6 Comparative Analysis: Inference Methods

| Inference Method | Direction | Data Required | Completeness | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward Chaining** | Data → Goal | All facts | Complete (definite clauses) | Production systems, monitoring |
| **Backward Chaining** | Goal → Data | Goal only | Complete (definite clauses) | Prolog, query answering |
| **Resolution** | Refutation | CNF KB | Refutation-complete | Theorem provers |
| **Model Checking** | Truth table | Bounded domain | Complete (finite) | Verification |
| **Propositionalization** | Reduce to PropL | Domain closure | Complete (decidable subset) | Small finite domains |
